1:
"Now the feast of unleavened bread drew nigh, which is called the Passover." The witnesses note the setting of the Passion at the Passover and its typical meaning. Chrysostom draws the contrast of the two Passovers: "the actions of the Jews were a shadow of our own; if you ask a Jew concerning the Passover, he will tell you of the deliverance from Egypt; but should a man inquire of me, he would hear of freedom from sin and the darkness of Satan, not by Moses, but by the Son of God." Bede explains the name: "the Passover, called in Hebrew Phase, is named not from the Passion but from the passing over, because the destroying angel, seeing the blood on the doors of the Israelites, passed over them, and touched not their first-born."

The witnesses note the providential timing: Christ would suffer at the very feast that prefigured his death, the true Passover at the type's appointed season.

So the witnesses make the verse the setting of the Passion at the Passover. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, the true Passover, would be sacrificed at the very feast that foreshadowed him. The witnesses note the fitness of the timing: the Passion drew near as the Passover drew nigh, so that the antitype should be offered at the season of the type. Chrysostom's contrast, the expositors press, opens the deeper meaning: the Jewish Passover commemorated the deliverance from Egypt by the blood of the lamb, but it was a shadow of a greater deliverance — the freedom from sin and from the bondage of Satan, won not by Moses but by the Son of God, the true Lamb. Bede's note on the name, the witnesses observe, draws out the type: as the destroying angel passed over the houses marked with the lamb's blood, sparing the first-born, so the blood of Christ, the true Passover Lamb, shelters his people from the wrath to come; the deliverance of Israel prefiguring the redemption of the Church. The expositors draw the lesson: the whole apparatus of the Jewish Passover — the lamb slain, the blood applied, the angel passing over — was a divinely appointed shadow of Christ's sacrifice, now to be fulfilled; and the providence that brought the Passion to the Passover declares that Christ is the substance of all the types, "our passover sacrificed for us" (1 Corinthians 5:7). The witnesses note the solemnity this lends to the narrative that follows: the events of the Last Supper and the cross are the fulfilment of centuries of shadow, the true Lamb of God now to be offered. The verse thus sets the Passion at the Passover, teaching that Christ is the true Passover, sacrificed at the season of the type, whose blood delivers his people from sin and death as the lamb's blood delivered Israel from the destroying angel.
2:
"And the chief priests and scribes sought how they might kill him; for they feared the people." The witnesses note the leaders' murderous plot and their fear of the people. The Reformed Gill notes the council met "not in their usual place the temple, but at the palace of Caiaphas the high priest." The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the imperfect "were seeking" — a continued, conative effort — and the verb "make away with" (anairein). Clarke observes the fear: "the great mass of the people seem to have been convinced that Christ was at least a prophet... the multitude who clamoured for his blood at the crucifixion appear to have been a mere mob, formed out of the creatures of the chief priests." The witnesses note the leaders sought to kill Christ, but feared to do it openly because of the people's attachment to him.

The witnesses note the plot held in check by fear: the rulers seeking Christ's death, but restrained by the people's regard for him.

So the witnesses make the verse the leaders' plot and its restraint. The lesson the expositors draw is the settled malice of the rulers against Christ and the providential restraint of their fear. The chief priests and scribes, the witnesses note, "sought how they might kill him" — their purpose fixed, their continued and earnest effort (the imperfect tense) bent on his destruction; they wanted not the will but the way. The expositors observe the obstacle that held them back: "for they feared the people." The great mass of the people, Clarke notes, regarded Christ as at least a prophet, and held to him; and the rulers dared not seize him openly, lest the people resist or riot. The witnesses press the providence in this restraint: God held back the malice of the enemies by the people's regard, so that Christ should not be taken before his hour, nor in a manner that would frustrate the appointed time and means of his death; the rulers' fear was the leash by which providence governed their plot. The expositors draw the lesson on the hypocrisy and cowardice of the leaders: they feared the people more than they feared God, and sought to compass their wicked design by stealth rather than openly; their fear was not of doing wrong but of the people's anger. The witnesses note Clarke's observation that the multitude later clamouring for Christ's blood was a mob got up by the priests — the people's attachment overborne, in the end, by the manipulation of the rulers; yet for the present, that attachment restrained the plot. The expositors observe how this fear shaped what follows: it made the leaders welcome Judas's offer to betray Christ privately, away from the multitude. The verse thus records the rulers' settled plot and its restraint by fear — they sought to kill Christ but feared the people — teaching the depth of their malice, the cowardice of their hypocrisy, and the providence that held their hands until the appointed hour, channelling their plot toward the secret betrayal that the next verses unfold.
3:
"Then entered Satan into Judas surnamed Iscariot, being of the number of the twelve." The witnesses note Satan's entrance and Judas's guilt. Titus of Bostra guards the freedom of the will: "Satan entered into Judas not by force, but finding the door open; for, forgetful of all he had seen, Judas now turned his thoughts solely to covetousness." Chrysostom notes the aggravation: Judas, "though partaking of the mystery, was not converted; his wickedness is made the more awful, that under the pollution of such a design he came to the mystery, and coming was not made better, either by fear, gratitude, or respect." The witnesses note the pointed phrase "of the number of the twelve" — the betrayer one of the chosen circle.

The witnesses note the dreadful turn: Satan entering Judas, who, though one of the twelve, opened his heart to covetousness and betrayal.

So the witnesses make the verse the entrance of Satan into the betrayer. The lesson the expositors draw is the awful conjunction of satanic temptation and human guilt, and the aggravation of betrayal by one of the chosen. The witnesses note that "Satan entered into Judas" — the devil taking possession of the traitor, prompting and energizing the betrayal; yet, Titus presses, not "by force, but finding the door open" — Judas was no helpless victim, but had opened his own heart to Satan by his settled covetousness, so that the devil entered where the will had already given way. The expositors draw the careful lesson: Satan tempts, but the sinner consents; the devil's entrance does not excuse Judas but exposes the open door of his own greed, which invited the tempter in. The witnesses observe the aggravation Chrysostom marks: Judas's wickedness is the more dreadful because he was no stranger but a disciple, one who had seen all Christ's works and heard all his words, and who (as the verses to come show) came to the holy mystery itself with the design of betrayal in his heart — unmoved by fear, gratitude, or reverence; the nearness of his privilege deepened the horror of his crime. The phrase "of the number of the twelve," the expositors note, is set down for contrast: the betrayer belonged to the first and chosen band, the apostolic college, though he did not truly discharge the apostolic office — the basest treachery springing from the inmost circle. The witnesses draw the solemn warning: the highest privilege is no security against the deepest fall; one who walked with Christ, saw his miracles, and shared his table, yet, opening his heart to covetousness, became the instrument of Satan and the betrayer of his Lord. The verse thus records Satan's entrance into Judas — by the open door of his greed — teaching the conjunction of temptation and guilt, the peril of covetousness, and the dreadful possibility that one of the chosen, unguarded, may become the agent of the devil.
4:
"And he went his way, and communed with the chief priests and captains, how he might betray him unto them." The witnesses note Judas's approach to the rulers and identify the "captains." The Reformed Gill notes Judas "went his way from Christ, and the rest of the apostles." Lightfoot examines the "captains" (called "captains of the temple," v.52): he argues they were not the Roman garrison of Antonia — for the Romans had not conspired against Christ — but "the rulers in the temple," the Jewish temple-officers; so Judas dealt not with the occupying power but with the temple authorities. The witnesses note Judas himself sought out the rulers, taking the initiative to arrange the betrayal.

The witnesses note Judas's deliberate action: going to the chief priests and temple-captains to negotiate the betrayal of his Master.

So the witnesses make the verse Judas's deliberate approach to the rulers. The lesson the expositors draw is the calculated treachery of Judas, who took the initiative in his crime. The witnesses note that Judas "went his way" — departing from Christ and the apostles to seek out the enemies; the betrayal was no sudden impulse but a deliberate, premeditated act, the traitor himself going to "commune with the chief priests and captains" to arrange how he might deliver Christ into their hands. The expositors observe Lightfoot's identification of the "captains": not the Roman officers of the Antonia garrison (who had no part in the plot against Christ), but the Jewish temple-authorities, the rulers and officers of the temple guard — so that Judas dealt with the religious establishment, the very leaders who sought Christ's death. The witnesses press the calculated nature of the treachery: Judas did not wait to be sought, nor act under sudden pressure, but went of his own accord to the rulers, offering and negotiating the betrayal — the cold deliberation of a heart given over to covetousness and Satan. The expositors draw the lesson on the progress of sin: the covetousness that opened the door to Satan (v.3) now issues in deliberate action; the traitor moves from inward consent to outward conspiracy, seeking out the enemies and arranging the deed. The witnesses note the dreadful contrast with his calling: the apostle, sent to preach Christ, now goes to betray him; the one who had been with Christ in all his works turns his knowledge of his Master's movements into the means of his arrest. The expositors observe the providence even here: the rulers, who feared to take Christ openly (v.2), find in Judas the very means they sought — one who could deliver Christ privately, away from the people. The verse thus records Judas's deliberate approach to the temple-rulers — going of his own will to arrange the betrayal — teaching the calculated treachery of the traitor, the progress of sin from consent to conspiracy, and the dark fitness by which the betrayer's own initiative supplied the rulers' need.
5:
"And they were glad, and covenanted to give him money." The witnesses note the rulers' gladness and the bargain. The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the "ingressive aorist" — "a natural exultation that one of the Twelve had offered to do this thing"; and notes Luke says only "money" (argyrion), not Matthew's "thirty pieces." Clarke adds that the thirty pieces of silver were "the common price of the meanest slave" (cf. Zechariah 11:12; Exodus 21:32). The witnesses note the rulers' joy at the offer and their agreement to pay for the betrayal.

The witnesses note the rulers' eager gladness: delighted that a disciple would betray Christ, and ready to pay for it.

So the witnesses make the verse the striking of the bargain. The lesson the expositors draw is the malicious joy of the rulers and the contemptible price set on the Son of God. The witnesses note the rulers "were glad" — and Robertson marks the natural exultation: they who had sought how to kill Christ but feared the people now rejoiced that one of his own twelve had offered to deliver him; their problem was solved, the betrayer come to them unsought. The expositors observe the dreadfulness of their gladness: they rejoiced at the prospect of murdering the innocent, and at the treachery of a disciple against his Master — a joy that exposes the depth of their malice. The witnesses press the bargain: they "covenanted to give him money" — a formal agreement, a price set on the betrayal; and the expositors note Clarke's observation that the thirty pieces (which Matthew names, though Luke says only "money") were the common price of the meanest slave (Exodus 21:32; Zechariah 11:12) — so that the Lord of glory was valued at the price of a slave, the cheapness of the sum heightening the insult. The witnesses observe the fulfilment of prophecy: Zechariah had foretold the thirty pieces of silver, the "goodly price" at which Christ was valued (Zechariah 11:12-13), now exactly accomplished. The expositors draw the lesson on covetousness: Judas sold his Lord for money, and the rulers bought him; the love of money, which had opened Judas's heart to Satan, now seals the bargain — the basest of motives in the basest of crimes. The witnesses note the contrast with the worth of Christ: the priceless Son of God, valued by his enemies at a slave's price, bought and sold like merchandise. The verse thus records the striking of the bargain — the rulers glad, the price agreed — teaching the malicious joy of the enemies, the contemptible valuing of Christ, the fulfilment of prophecy in the thirty pieces, and the covetousness that bought and sold the Lord of glory.
6:
"And he promised, and sought opportunity to betray him unto them in the absence of the multitude." The witnesses note Judas's binding promise and his search for a chance. The Reformed Gill explains he "undertook to deliver him into their hands... he laid himself under obligation to do it." The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the phrase "in the absence of the multitude" is "perhaps nearer the meaning, without a tumult." The witnesses note Judas now watched for the right moment — when Christ should be apart from the crowds — to deliver him without the disturbance the rulers feared.

The witnesses note Judas's settled commitment and his patient watching for the opportunity to betray Christ quietly.

So the witnesses make the verse the sealing of Judas's commitment and his search for opportunity. The lesson the expositors draw is the cold deliberation of the traitor, bound by promise and watching for his chance. The witnesses note that Judas "promised" — he bound himself by a solemn undertaking to deliver Christ; the bargain was sealed on both sides, the rulers paying and Judas pledging. The expositors observe the settled resolve this marks: the betrayal was no longer a mere temptation or impulse, but a contracted obligation; Judas had given his word and taken his price, and now stood committed to the deed. And he "sought opportunity," the witnesses press — patiently watching, waiting for the fitting moment to betray Christ "in the absence of the multitude," that is (as Ellicott notes), without a tumult, away from the crowds whose attachment the rulers feared. The expositors draw the lesson on the patience of evil: Judas did not act rashly, but bided his time, looking for the chance to deliver Christ quietly, when he should be apart from the people — the cold calculation of a traitor serving both his own greed and the rulers' fear. The witnesses note the dreadful steadiness of the betrayer: from this point he moved among the apostles as before, sharing their fellowship and (as the verses show) their last supper, all the while watching for the opportunity to deliver his Master — the deepest hypocrisy, the smiling traitor with the bargain sealed in his heart. The expositors observe the providence that shaped even this: the opportunity Judas sought — Christ apart from the multitude, in Gethsemane by night — was the very means by which the appointed arrest would come, in the manner and at the hour God had ordained. The verse thus records the sealing of Judas's commitment and his patient search for opportunity — bound by promise, watching for his chance — teaching the cold deliberation of the traitor, the patience of evil, and the dark fitness by which his watching served the appointed hour of the Passion.
7:
"Then came the day of unleavened bread, when the passover must be killed." The witnesses note the arrival of the Passover and Christ's fulfilment of the type. Titus of Bostra draws the purpose: "our Lord, in order to leave us a heavenly Passover, ate a typical one, removing the figure, that the truth might take its place." Bede identifies the day: "the fourteenth day of the first month, on which, having put away the leaven, they were accustomed to hold the Passover, that is, the lamb, towards evening." Eusebius addresses the chronological question, noting Christ did not keep the Passover at the same hour the Jews sacrificed the lamb (they did so on the Preparation, when Christ suffered). The witnesses note the appointed day come, when the Passover lamb must be slain.

The witnesses note the day of the type's fulfilment: Christ keeping the typical Passover as he was about to become the true one.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrival of the Passover and the meeting of type and antitype. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ kept the old Passover even as he was about to fulfil and abolish it, the figure giving way to the truth. The witnesses note the solemn timing: "the day of unleavened bread, when the passover must be killed" had come — the appointed day for slaying the lamb, the fourteenth of the first month (Bede); and on this very day Christ, the true Passover Lamb, would keep the typical feast and then be offered himself. Titus's observation, the expositors press, captures the meaning: Christ "ate a typical Passover, removing the figure, that the truth might take its place" — he observed the old rite to fulfil it, and by fulfilling it, abolished it, substituting (as the institution of the Supper would show) the new Passover of his own body and blood for the old lamb. The witnesses observe the chronological care of the fathers: Eusebius notes that Christ did not keep his Passover at the same hour the Jews sacrificed the lamb — they did so on the Preparation, the very time Christ suffered on the cross; so that, in the deepest sense, Christ the true Lamb was slain at the hour the typical lambs were being killed, the antitype offered as the type was fulfilled. The expositors draw the lesson: the convergence of the old Passover and the Passion is no accident but the design of God, who would have his Son offered at the season and (in the reckoning of the cross) the hour of the type, that the fulfilment might be unmistakable — Christ "our passover sacrificed for us" (1 Corinthians 5:7). The witnesses note the transition this marks: the old covenant feast comes to its last observance and its fulfilment together, as Christ prepares to institute the new. The verse thus records the arrival of the Passover — the day the lamb must be slain — teaching that Christ kept the old feast to fulfil and abolish it, the figure yielding to the truth, as the true Lamb prepared to be offered at the season of the type.
8:
"And he sent Peter and John, saying, Go and prepare us the passover, that we may eat." The witnesses note the sending of the two disciples, named only by Luke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes: "St. Luke's is the only Gospel that gives the names of the two disciples; they were together now, as they were afterwards" (John 20:3; 21:20; Acts 3:1) — "we may perhaps recognise the purpose of a loving insight in the act which thus brought the two disciples together at a time when our Lord foresaw how much one would need the love and sympathy of the other." The witnesses note Christ's direction that the Passover be prepared, sending the two trusted disciples to make it ready.

The witnesses note the sending of Peter and John together — the two whom Luke alone names — to prepare the Passover.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of the two disciples to prepare the Passover. The lesson the expositors draw is Christ's orderly provision for the feast and the loving wisdom in his choice of the two. The witnesses note that Christ "sent Peter and John" — the two leading and beloved disciples — to "prepare the passover," making ready the lamb, the unleavened bread, and all that the feast required; the Lord, though about to suffer, attends to the orderly keeping of the appointed feast, providing for its due celebration. The expositors observe the detail peculiar to Luke, which Ellicott draws out: of the four Gospels, only Luke names the two disciples as Peter and John; and Ellicott finds in this a touch of loving insight — these two, so often together (at the empty tomb, in the temple, in Acts), are joined here by Christ at the very time when, as the Lord foresaw, the one (Peter) would soon need the love and sympathy of the other after his fall. The witnesses press the tenderness this suggests: Christ, knowing Peter's coming denial and grief, may have bound him to John, the disciple of love, that Peter might have a friend's heart near him in his bitter hour. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's care: even in arranging the practical preparation of the feast, the Lord acts with foresight and love, joining those who would need each other; nothing in his ordering is careless or chance. The witnesses note the trust reposed in the two: the most important preparation, for the last Passover and the institution of the Supper, is committed to Peter and John, the chief of the apostles. The verse thus records the sending of Peter and John to prepare the Passover — named by Luke alone — teaching Christ's orderly provision for the feast and the loving wisdom in his choice, joining the two who would need each other, and committing to his chief disciples the preparation of the night on which he would institute the Supper and be betrayed.
9:
"And they said unto him, Where wilt thou that we prepare?" The witnesses note the disciples' question about the place. The Reformed Gill explains they did not ask in what town — "for it was a fixed and determined thing that the passover should be eaten at Jerusalem, and nowhere else" (Deuteronomy 16:5-6) — but in what house in Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the deliberative subjunctive, "Where wilt thou that we make ready?" The witnesses note the disciples, ready to obey, ask only where the preparation should be made.

The witnesses note the disciples' simple, obedient question: not whether, but where, to prepare the Passover.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' obedient inquiry. The lesson the expositors draw is the ready obedience of the disciples and the providential ordering that would direct them to the place. The witnesses note the form of their question: "Where wilt thou that we prepare?" — they do not question the command, nor ask whether they should prepare, but only where; their obedience is prompt and entire, awaiting only the Master's direction as to the place. The expositors observe Gill's clarification: the question was not about the town, for the law fixed that the Passover must be eaten at Jerusalem and nowhere else (Deuteronomy 16); the disciples knew the city, and asked only in which house within it the feast should be made ready. The witnesses press the readiness this shows: the disciples are willing servants, eager to do their Master's will, needing only to know the appointed place; their question is the question of obedience, not of reluctance or doubt. The expositors draw the lesson, which the following verses unfold: Christ would direct them to a place they did not know, by a sign he would give — showing his foreknowledge and his sovereign provision; the disciples' part was to ask and obey, the Lord's to direct. The witnesses note the contrast with Judas: while the traitor sought opportunity to betray Christ, the faithful disciples sought direction to serve him; the same hours that held Judas's treachery held Peter and John's obedience. The expositors observe the quiet trust in the disciples' question: they did not know where the Passover would be kept, nor how Christ would arrange it, but trusted him to direct them, and asked simply "Where?" The verse thus records the disciples' obedient inquiry — where to prepare — teaching their ready willingness to do Christ's will, the providential ordering that would direct them, and the contrast between the faithful disciples seeking to serve and the traitor seeking to betray; and it prepares for the sign by which Christ would lead them to the upper room.
10:
"And he said unto them, Behold, when ye are entered into the city, there shall a man meet you, bearing a pitcher of water; follow him into the house where he entereth in." The witnesses note the sign Christ gave and his foreknowledge. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes "the signal is one of the details common to St. Mark and St. Luke," and Robertson explains the genitive absolute and the verb "meet you." The witnesses note the precise sign — a man bearing a pitcher of water (an unusual sight, water-carrying being women's work) — by which the disciples would find the house, a token of Christ's exact foreknowledge.

The witnesses note the sign of foreknowledge: the man with the pitcher, by whom the disciples would be led to the appointed house.

So the witnesses make the verse the sign by which Christ directed the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is the foreknowledge and sovereign provision of Christ, who knew and arranged every detail of the place. The witnesses note the precise direction: the disciples, on entering the city, would be met by "a man bearing a pitcher of water" — and they were to follow him to the house he entered. The expositors observe the exactness of the sign: that a particular man, carrying water (a task usually left to women, so the sight conspicuous), would meet them at the moment of their entering — a detail no human foresight could arrange, proving Christ's divine knowledge of things future and distant. The witnesses press the same wonder seen in the colt of the triumphal entry: Christ knows beforehand the very persons, places, and circumstances, and orders them to his purpose; nothing is hidden from him, and all things serve his will. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's sovereign provision: he had prepared the place, perhaps by his Spirit moving the householder, perhaps by his foreknowledge of the man's errand; and he directs his disciples to it by an unmistakable sign, that they might find it without fail. The witnesses note the reason, drawn out in the verses to come, why Christ did not simply name the house: that Judas, watching for opportunity to betray him, might not know the place beforehand, and so could not deliver Christ before the feast; the secrecy of the sign guarded the appointed hour. The expositors observe the obedience required of the disciples: they were to follow a stranger on the strength of Christ's word, trusting his direction — and (as the next verses show) found all exactly as he said. The verse thus records the sign of the pitcher-bearer — by which Christ led the disciples to the house — teaching his exact foreknowledge, his sovereign provision, and the wise secrecy that hid the place from the traitor until the appointed time.
11:
"And ye shall say unto the goodman of the house, The Master saith unto thee, Where is the guestchamber, where I shall eat the passover with my disciples?" The witnesses note the message to the householder and the title "the Master." The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains "the Master" as "literally, the Teacher — the Rabbi whom the man acknowledged," and Robertson glosses "goodman of the house" (oikodespotēs, master of the house). The witnesses note Christ claims, with quiet authority, a place to keep the Passover, the householder evidently a disciple who would know and own "the Master."

The witnesses note the authoritative message: "the Master" requests the guest-chamber, the householder ready to provide it.

So the witnesses make the verse the claiming of the upper room. The lesson the expositors draw is the authority of Christ and the readiness of a disciple to receive him. The witnesses note the message the disciples were to give: "The Master saith unto thee, Where is the guestchamber?" — Christ asks for the room, but with the authority of one who has a right to it; and the title "the Master" (the Teacher, the Rabbi) implies, the expositors observe, that the householder knew and owned Christ as his teacher, a disciple who would gladly give his guest-chamber for the feast. The expositors draw out the quiet majesty of the request: Christ does not beg but claims — "where is the guestchamber where I shall eat?" — speaking as one whose word is sufficient warrant, much as he claimed the colt ("the Lord hath need of him"); all things being at the disposal of the Master. The witnesses press the readiness of the householder, implied by the narrative: he needed only to hear that "the Master" required the room, and would provide it — a disciple's heart open to receive Christ, his house at his Lord's service. The expositors note the providence in this: Christ had a friend in Jerusalem, a disciple with a furnished upper room, ready at his word — God's provision for the feast, prepared beforehand. The witnesses observe the fitness of the place: a "guestchamber," an upper room, set apart and furnished, for the keeping of the last Passover and the institution of the Supper — the room where the new covenant feast would be ordained. The expositors draw the lesson for every disciple: to hold all that we have at the Master's disposal, ready, like this householder, to give our best for his service when he requires it; and to own him, as the householder did, as "the Master" whose word commands our glad obedience. The verse thus records the claiming of the upper room — "the Master" requesting the guest-chamber — teaching Christ's authority, the readiness of a disciple to receive him, and the providence that prepared the place for the great feast.
12:
"And he shall shew you a large upper room furnished: there make ready." The witnesses note the furnished room provided for the feast. The Reformed Gill notes it was furnished "with all things necessary, to eat and drink out of, at the passover." The grammatical-historical Robertson explains the "large upper room" (cf. Mark 14:15). The witnesses note the room was ready, large, and furnished — fitly prepared for Christ and the twelve to keep the Passover and institute the Supper.

The witnesses note the provision: a large, furnished upper room, made ready at Christ's word for the feast.

So the witnesses make the verse the provision of the upper room. The lesson the expositors draw is the providence of God in preparing a fit place for the great feast, and the disciples' part in making it ready. The witnesses note the description: "a large upper room furnished" — spacious enough for Christ and the twelve, and already furnished with the couches and vessels needed for the Passover; the householder would show it, and there the disciples were to "make ready." The expositors observe the fitness of the place that providence had prepared: a large room (for the company), an upper room (set apart, retired), furnished (ready for use) — exactly suited for the keeping of the last Passover and the solemn institution of the Lord's Supper. The witnesses press the providence in the provision: Christ found, at his word, just such a room as the occasion required, prepared beforehand by a disciple's readiness and God's ordering — a striking instance of how the Lord provides for his own work, even to the room in which it shall be done. The expositors draw the lesson on the disciples' part: though the room was furnished, they were still to "make ready" — to prepare the lamb, the bread, the wine, and all the feast required; providence provides the place, but the servants do the work. The witnesses note the spiritual application often drawn: as the upper room was prepared and furnished for Christ, so the heart is to be made a fit dwelling for him — a large room, furnished with grace, swept and garnished, where the Lord may keep his feast with his people. The expositors observe the solemnity of what this room would witness: the last Passover, the washing of the disciples' feet, the institution of the Supper, the unmasking of the traitor, the great discourses — all in this furnished upper room, providence having prepared the stage for the most sacred night. The verse thus records the provision of the upper room — large and furnished, ready at Christ's word — teaching the providence that prepares a fit place for his work, the disciples' part in making it ready, and the fitness of the chamber for the great feast about to be kept there.
13:
"And they went, and found as he had said unto them: and they made ready the passover." The witnesses note the exact fulfilment of Christ's word and the disciples' obedience. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the past perfect "he had said" (cf. Mark 14:16), marking the precise correspondence. The witnesses note the disciples went, found everything exactly as Christ had foretold, and prepared the Passover — their obedience rewarded with the confirmation of his word.

The witnesses note the confirmation: all came to pass as Christ said, and the disciples made the feast ready.

So the witnesses make the verse the exact fulfilment of Christ's direction. The lesson the expositors draw is the confirmation of Christ's foreknowledge by the precise fulfilment, and the blessing of obedient faith. The witnesses note that the disciples "went, and found as he had said unto them" — everything exactly as Christ had foretold: the man with the pitcher, the householder, the furnished upper room; not a detail failed, the word confirmed to the letter. The expositors press the proof this affords of Christ's divine foreknowledge: the disciples, obeying a direction that depended wholly on circumstances beyond human control, found all precisely as predicted — a token of the omniscience of the One they served, who knew and ordered the future. The witnesses observe the disciples' simple obedience: they "went" at Christ's word, following a sign they could not have arranged, trusting his direction; and their obedience was rewarded with the finding of all as he said, and the privilege of preparing the great feast. The expositors draw the lesson on obedient faith: the disciple who acts on Christ's word, even where he cannot see the outcome, finds it made good; the word of Christ never fails, and faith that obeys is never disappointed — the disciples found "as he had said," as Christ's people in every age find his promises fulfilled. The witnesses note the quiet conclusion: "they made ready the passover" — the preparation done, the lamb slain and roasted, the bread and wine set, the table furnished, all in readiness for the most solemn supper ever kept. The expositors observe the providence and the foreknowledge converging: the place prepared, the sign fulfilled, the feast made ready — the stage set, by Christ's word and the disciples' obedience, for the institution of the Lord's Supper. The verse thus records the exact fulfilment of Christ's direction and the disciples' obedience — they found as he said, and made ready — teaching the confirmation of his foreknowledge, the reward of obedient faith, and the completion of the preparation for the great feast on the night he was betrayed.
14:
"And when the hour was come, he sat down, and the twelve apostles with him." The witnesses note the solemn hour and Christ's posture. Bede identifies "the hour of eating the Passover" as "the fourteenth day far gone towards evening." Theophylact addresses how Christ "sat down" (reclined) though the Passover was eaten standing: "when they had eaten the legal Passover, they sat down according to the common custom, to eat their other food." The witnesses note Christ and the twelve gathered at the appointed hour for the last supper — the betrayer among them.

The witnesses note the solemn gathering: Christ reclining with the twelve at the appointed hour of the feast.

So the witnesses make the verse the gathering for the Last Supper. The lesson the expositors draw is the solemnity of the appointed hour and the gathering of Christ with his apostles for the feast that would become the Supper. The witnesses note the phrase "when the hour was come" — the appointed time, fixed by God, for the keeping of the Passover and the institution of the new feast; the hour of which the whole narrative has spoken, drawing near with solemn weight. The expositors observe Bede's marking of the time — the fourteenth day toward evening, the hour of the Passover — and Theophylact's resolution of how Christ "sat down" (reclined) though the Passover was originally eaten standing (Exodus 12:11): the custom by Christ's day was to eat the lamb standing, then recline for the rest of the meal; so Christ and the apostles, having kept the legal rite, reclined at table for the supper that followed. The witnesses press the company gathered: "the twelve apostles with him" — the inner circle, the chosen band, gathered around their Lord for this last and most sacred meal; yet among them, the expositors note with sorrow, was Judas the betrayer, reclining at the table with the bargain sealed in his heart, sharing the feast he meant to turn to treachery. The expositors draw the lesson on the solemnity of the scene: this was Christ's last supper with his own before his Passion, the hour he had "desired with desire" (as the next verse declares); a moment of deep tenderness and gravity, the Lord gathered with his apostles on the eve of his death. The witnesses note the providence that brought all to this hour: the preparation done, the place ready, the company assembled, the appointed time come — all converging for the institution of the Supper and the events of the sacred night. The verse thus records the gathering for the Last Supper — Christ reclining with the twelve at the appointed hour — teaching the solemnity of the moment, the tenderness of the Lord's last meal with his own, and the dark presence of the traitor among the chosen at the holy feast.
15:
"And he said unto them, With desire I have desired to eat this passover with you before I suffer." The witnesses note Christ's earnest longing for this last Passover. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains "with desire I have desired" as "a Hebrew form of expression, meaning I have greatly desired," and offers reasons: that Christ, about to leave them, wished once to partake with them of the religious privilege; that he would prepare them for his sufferings; and that, being man as well as God, he valued the blessings of social and religious fellowship. Cyril notes Christ had hidden the place from Judas precisely so that "he might not betray Him before the feast." Theophylact reads it as the word of one departing: "This is My last supper with you, therefore it is most precious and welcome to Me."

The witnesses note Christ's deep longing: he had earnestly desired to keep this last Passover with his disciples before his Passion.

So the witnesses make the verse the expression of Christ's earnest longing. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender love of Christ for his disciples, displayed in his earnest desire for this last fellowship before his death. The witnesses note the intensity of the Hebrew idiom — "with desire I have desired" — meaning, as Barnes explains, that Christ had greatly, earnestly longed to eat this Passover with them; the phrase breathes a deep and affectionate yearning. The expositors observe the reasons for this longing. Theophylact reads it as the word of one about to depart: this was Christ's "last supper" with his own, and therefore most precious to him — the final meal of fellowship before the parting of the cross. Barnes adds the further grounds: Christ, truly man, valued the blessings of religious and social fellowship, and wished once more to partake with his disciples of the appointed feast; and he desired the occasion to prepare them for his sufferings and to institute the new feast in place of the old. The witnesses press the tenderness this reveals: the Lord, on the eve of his agony, with the betrayer at the table and the cross before him, turns to his disciples with a heart full of love, longing for this last hour of fellowship — his thoughts not on his own coming sufferings only, but on his communion with his own. Cyril's note, the expositors observe, adds a touch of the providence: Christ had kept the place secret from Judas, that he might not be betrayed before the feast, so that this desired supper might be kept — his longing fulfilled, the traitor's opportunity deferred until the meal was done. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's love: the desire for this Passover is the desire of a love that, "having loved his own, loved them unto the end" (John 13:1) — a love that treasured the last fellowship and would seal it with the gift of the Supper. The verse thus records Christ's earnest longing for the last Passover — "with desire I have desired" — teaching the tender love of the Lord for his disciples, his valuing of holy fellowship, and the affection with which he approached the last supper before his suffering.
16:
"For I say unto you, I will not any more eat thereof, until it be fulfilled in the kingdom of God." The witnesses note Christ's farewell to the Passover and its fulfilment in the kingdom. The Reformed Gill notes the Passover "now, with the rest of the ceremonial law, was to be abolished." Clarke explains "until it be fulfilled" as "until that of which the passover is a type is fulfilled in my death, through which the kingdom of God shall be established." Ellicott finds a depth: "the Passover was fulfilled in the kingdom of God (1) in the sacrifice on the cross; (2) in every commemoration of that sacrifice" — every such act a communion with Christ. The witnesses note Christ's word that this was his last Passover until its fulfilment in the kingdom.

The witnesses note Christ's farewell to the old feast: he would eat it no more until it was fulfilled in the kingdom of God.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's farewell to the Passover and its fulfilment. The lesson the expositors draw is that the old Passover finds its fulfilment in Christ's sacrifice and in the kingdom of God, the type giving way to the antitype. The witnesses note Christ's solemn farewell: "I will not any more eat thereof, until it be fulfilled" — this was his last keeping of the old Passover, which, with the ceremonial law, was now to be abolished, its purpose accomplished in him. The expositors press the meaning of "fulfilled in the kingdom of God." Clarke reads it of the cross: the Passover, a type, would be fulfilled in Christ's death, the true Passover sacrifice, through which the kingdom of God would be established. Ellicott opens a fuller depth: the Passover is fulfilled in the kingdom both in the sacrifice on the cross (the one offering of the true Lamb) and in every commemoration of that sacrifice in the Supper, where the disciples have communion with the crucified Christ. The witnesses observe the convergence: the old feast, which prefigured redemption, gives way to the new — the cross fulfilling the type, and the Supper perpetuating its memory and communion; so that the Passover passes into the Lord's Supper, the shadow into the substance. The expositors draw the lesson on the transition of the covenants: Christ keeps the last Passover and bids it farewell, for its work is done; henceforth the new feast of his body and blood takes its place, until the final consummation in the kingdom, when (as some read) the feast shall be perfected in the marriage supper of the Lamb. The witnesses note the forward look: Christ's word points beyond the cross to the kingdom, the present communion to the future glory, where the saints shall feast with him for ever. The verse thus records Christ's farewell to the Passover and its fulfilment in the kingdom — the old feast ending, the new beginning, the type fulfilled in the cross and the Supper — teaching the transition from shadow to substance, and the perfecting of the feast in the kingdom of God.
17:
"And he took the cup, and gave thanks, and said, Take this, and divide it among yourselves." The witnesses note this cup and the distinctive order of Luke's account. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that Luke's arrangement places a cup before the institution of the bread, and discusses the textual and liturgical questions this raises. Adam Clarke notes the manner: "it does not appear that our Lord handed either the bread or the cup to each person; he gave it to him next to him, and by handing it from one to another, they shared it among themselves." The witnesses note Christ took a cup, gave thanks, and bade the disciples divide it — a cup of the Passover, distinct (in Luke's ordering) from the cup of the new covenant that follows.

The witnesses note the first cup: Christ giving thanks and bidding the disciples share it among themselves.

So the witnesses make the verse the first cup of the supper. The lesson the expositors draw is the thanksgiving and fellowship of the feast, and the careful ordering of Luke's account. The witnesses note that Christ "took the cup, and gave thanks" — the giving of thanks (the eucharistia) that marked the Passover cups, hallowing the feast with gratitude to God; and bade the disciples "Take this, and divide it among yourselves" — a cup shared in common, a token of their fellowship together and with their Lord. The expositors observe the question of order that Luke's account raises, which Ellicott discusses: Luke seems to record a cup here, before the institution of the bread, and then the cup of the new covenant after (v.20); most take this first cup to be one of the Passover cups (the feast having several), distinct from the eucharistic cup that follows — so that Luke records both the Passover cup and the covenant cup, where Matthew and Mark give only the latter. The witnesses note Clarke's observation on the manner of sharing: Christ did not hand the cup to each, but gave it to the one next him, and they passed it from hand to hand, dividing it among themselves — a picture of the communion of the disciples, sharing the one cup. The expositors draw the lesson on thanksgiving: Christ, even on the eve of his suffering, gives thanks over the cup — teaching his people to receive every blessing with gratitude, and to keep the feast with thanksgiving. The witnesses observe the fellowship the shared cup expresses: the disciples, dividing the one cup, are bound together in communion, one with another and with their Lord — a foretaste of the communion the Supper would seal. The expositors note the reverence of the scene: the Lord giving thanks, the disciples sharing the cup, in the solemn keeping of the last Passover before the new feast is instituted. The verse thus records the first cup — Christ giving thanks, the disciples dividing it — teaching thanksgiving and fellowship in the feast, and marking the careful order of Luke's account, in which the Passover cup precedes the cup of the new covenant.
18:
"For I say unto you, I will not drink of the fruit of the vine, until the kingdom of God shall come." The witnesses note Christ's farewell to the cup and a textual point of order. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes "of the fruit of the vine — better, of the product," and observes that here the words precede the institution, where in Matthew and Mark they follow; he judges Luke's arrangement (parallel to the like words on the Passover, v.16) "on the whole in favour." Clarke reads "until the kingdom of God shall come" of the near establishment of the gospel kingdom: "before another passover, the Holy Ghost shall descend, the Gospel be established, and the sacramental supper take the place of the paschal lamb." The witnesses note Christ's word that he would drink no more of the cup until the kingdom came.

The witnesses note Christ's second farewell — to the cup, as to the Passover — until the coming of the kingdom.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's farewell to the cup. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, taking leave of the old feast, looks forward to the coming of the kingdom in which all is fulfilled. The witnesses note the parallel with verse 16: as Christ said he would eat no more of the Passover until it was fulfilled, so now he says he will "not drink of the fruit of the vine, until the kingdom of God shall come" — a second farewell, to the cup as to the lamb, marking the end of the old feast and the looking-forward to the new. The expositors observe Ellicott's note on the order: Luke places this saying before the institution of the Supper, parallel to the like word on the Passover, where Matthew and Mark place it after; and the parallelism, Ellicott judges, favours Luke's arrangement — Christ bidding farewell to both elements of the old feast before instituting the new. The witnesses press the meaning of "until the kingdom of God shall come." Clarke reads it of the near establishment of the gospel kingdom: before another Passover came round, the Spirit would descend, the gospel be established, and the Lord's Supper take the place of the paschal lamb — so that Christ would, in a sense, drink anew with his people in the kingdom now to be set up. Others extend it to the final consummation, when Christ shall drink it new with his own in the Father's kingdom (Matthew 26:29) — the marriage supper of the Lamb. The witnesses observe the forward look that both readings share: Christ's farewell to the cup is not an end but a looking-forward — to the kingdom, in which the fellowship of the feast shall be renewed and perfected. The expositors draw the lesson on the hope: even in bidding farewell, Christ points his disciples to the joy to come, the kingdom in which they shall feast with him; the parting of the cross is the prelude to the reunion of the kingdom. The verse thus records Christ's farewell to the cup — he would drink no more until the kingdom came — teaching the end of the old feast, the hope of the kingdom, and the forward look to the renewed and perfected fellowship with Christ.
19:
"And he took bread, and gave thanks, and brake it, and gave unto them, saying, This is my body which is given for you: this do in remembrance of me." The witnesses note the institution of the Lord's Supper and the great eucharistic debate. Bede frames it: "having finished the rites of the old Passover, He passes to the new, which He desires the Church to celebrate in memory of His redemption, substituting for the flesh and blood of the lamb the Sacrament of His own Flesh and Blood in the figure of the bread and wine." The fathers press the real presence: Gregory of Nyssa — "the bread before the consecration is common bread, but when the mystery has consecrated it, it is, and is called, the Body of Christ"; Cyril — "doubt not that this is true, for He plainly says, This is my body; receive the words of your Saviour in faith." Yet the words "this do in remembrance of me" ground the commemorative reading the Reformed press: the Supper a memorial of Christ's sacrifice once offered.

The witnesses note Christ's institution of the Supper: the bread his body "given for you," to be done in remembrance of him.

So the witnesses make the verse the institution of the Lord's Supper, the heart of the chapter. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ ordained the Supper as the memorial and means of communion in his body broken for his people — though the manner of his presence has long divided the witnesses. The witnesses note the solemn acts: Christ "took bread, and gave thanks, and brake it, and gave" — the same fourfold action (took, blessed, brake, gave) by which he had fed the multitudes, now hallowing the bread of the new feast; and Bede notes the significance of the breaking: he breaks the bread to show that the breaking of his body, his Passion, would not be without his will. The words "This is my body which is given for you," the expositors observe, declare the meaning of the bread: it represents (or, the fathers say, is) his body, given — offered in sacrifice — "for you," for his people's redemption. Here the great debate among the witnesses opens. The fathers, the witnesses note, press a real presence: Gregory of Nyssa and Cyril hold that the consecrated bread, by the working of the mystery, is and is called the body of Christ, and bid the believer receive the words in faith, not doubting; the Latin and Eastern tradition reads "this is my body" as effecting what it signifies. The Reformed and many later witnesses, the expositors observe, read the words figuratively — "this is my body" meaning "this represents my body," as Scripture often uses "is" for "signifies" — and ground the Supper's purpose in the command "this do in remembrance of me": the Supper a memorial of Christ's one sacrifice, a showing-forth of his death, and a means of spiritual communion with him by faith, not a repetition of the offering nor a corporal eating. The witnesses note that all agree, whatever their difference on the manner, that Christ instituted the Supper as the memorial of his sacrifice and the means of communion in his body given for his people. The expositors draw the lesson on the love and the design of Christ: in the very night of his betrayal, he gave his people this feast — the pledge of his body broken for them, the memorial of his redeeming death, the means of their communion with him — that they might remember him, feed on him by faith, and show forth his death till he come. The verse thus records the institution of the Supper — the bread his body given, to be done in remembrance — the memorial and communion of the Church, on whose deepest meaning the witnesses have long differed.
20:
"Likewise also the cup after supper, saying, This cup is the new testament in my blood, which is shed for you." The witnesses note the cup of the new covenant. The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke paraphrases: "this cup which is poured out for you signifies the blood of the new covenant, which is shortly to be ratified by the shedding of my blood"; or, "the paschal sacrifice and my sacrifice happen together." Ellicott and Gill note the cup, taken "after supper," is the cup of the new testament (covenant) in Christ's blood, distinct from the earlier Passover cup. The witnesses note the cup represents the blood of the new covenant, shed for the remission of sins.

The witnesses note the cup of the new covenant: Christ's blood shed for his people, sealing the new testament.

So the witnesses make the verse the institution of the cup. The lesson the expositors draw is that the cup represents the blood of Christ, by which the new covenant is ratified for the remission of sins. The witnesses note the time — "the cup after supper" — the cup taken after the meal, distinct (in Luke's order) from the earlier Passover cup; this is the cup of the new covenant, the second element of the Supper. The words "This cup is the new testament in my blood," the expositors observe, declare its meaning: the cup represents Christ's blood, by which the "new testament" or new covenant is established — the covenant of grace, promising forgiveness of sins and reconciliation with God, ratified and sealed by the shedding of Christ's blood. Clarke's paraphrase, the witnesses note, draws out the sense: the cup signifies the blood of the new covenant, about to be shed on the cross; and the same eucharistic debate that attends the bread (v.19) attends the cup — the fathers reading a real presence of the blood, the Reformed a representative sign. The expositors press the great truth on which all agree: the new covenant is founded on the blood of Christ, "shed for you" — poured out in sacrifice for his people, for the remission of their sins (as Matthew adds, 26:28); the cup is the seal and pledge of that covenant, the token that by his blood the believer's sins are forgiven and the new covenant established. The witnesses observe the richness of "the new testament in my blood": as the old covenant was ratified with the blood of sacrifices (Exodus 24:8), so the new is ratified with the precious blood of Christ — the better covenant founded on the better sacrifice (Hebrews 9). The expositors draw the lesson on the comfort and the call: the cup assures the believer that Christ's blood was shed for him, his sins remitted, the covenant sealed; and it calls him to remember and to feed by faith on the blood of his Redeemer. The witnesses note the completeness of the Supper now instituted — the bread (the body) and the cup (the blood) — the whole memorial and communion of Christ's sacrifice, given to the Church on the night of his betrayal. The verse thus records the institution of the cup — the new covenant in Christ's blood, shed for his people — teaching that the new covenant is sealed by his blood, that the cup is its pledge of forgiveness, and completing the Supper that the Church should keep in remembrance of him.
21:
"But, behold, the hand of him that betrayeth me is with me on the table." The witnesses note Christ's revelation of the betrayer at the very table. Augustine notes the placing: "when our Lord had given the cup to His disciples, He again spoke of His betrayer." Theophylact draws the lesson of Christ's goodness: "He left nothing undone that belonged to Him to do, for He gives us an example that even to the end we should be employed in reclaiming sinners; and moreover to point out the baseness of the traitor who blushed not to be His guest." Bede notes Christ "does not especially point him out, lest being so plainly detected he might become the more shameless, but throws the charge on the whole twelve, that the guilty one might be turned to repentance." The witnesses note the betrayer's hand on the table with Christ's.

The witnesses note the dreadful nearness: the betrayer's hand sharing the table and the feast with Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's revelation of the betrayer at the table. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Judas's treachery, the patience of Christ in seeking even his repentance, and the warning against betraying the Lord whose table we share. The witnesses note the timing Augustine marks: it was after the cup, after the holy feast, that Christ spoke of his betrayer — "the hand of him that betrayeth me is with me on the table"; the traitor had shared the supper, his hand reaching to the same dish, even as the bargain lay sealed in his heart. The expositors press the baseness this reveals (Theophylact): Judas "blushed not to be His guest" — he ate at Christ's table, received his kindness, shared the fellowship of the feast, all the while purposing the betrayal; the deepest hypocrisy, the smiling traitor at the sacred meal. The witnesses observe the patience and goodness of Christ that the fathers note: he reveals the betrayer not to expose and shame him beyond recovery, but (Bede) without naming him, throwing the charge on the whole twelve, that the guilty one might yet be turned to repentance; Christ, to the very end, seeks the salvation even of his betrayer, leaving nothing undone to reclaim him. The expositors draw the lesson Theophylact presses: Christ's example teaches that we should labour, even to the end, for the reclaiming of sinners, not despairing of any. The witnesses note the solemn warning the verse holds: that one may share Christ's table, partake of holy things, and yet betray him in the heart; the nearness of privilege is no proof of fidelity, and the hand that shares the feast may be the hand that betrays. The expositors observe the sorrow of Christ, who, at the feast of his love, must speak of the treachery in the midst of his own. The verse thus records Christ's revelation of the betrayer at the table — his hand sharing the feast — teaching the depth of Judas's hypocrisy, the patient goodness of Christ seeking even his repentance, and the warning that the sharing of holy things is no security against the betrayal of the Lord.
22:
"And truly the Son of man goeth, as it was determined: but woe unto that man by whom he is betrayed!" The witnesses note the joining of divine determination and human guilt. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the balance: "as it hath been determined (the perfect passive participle of horizō)... but this fact does not absolve Judas of his guilt as the woe here makes plain." Ellicott notes "as it was determined" is "eminently characteristic of St. Luke" (cf. Acts 2:23), and that the "woe" occurs in all three Gospels, an indelible impression. Clarke glosses "the Son of man goeth" as "is about to die." The witnesses note Christ holds together both truths: his death was determined by God, yet the betrayer is guilty.

The witnesses note the two truths joined: Christ's death foreordained, yet Judas's betrayal damnable.

So the witnesses make the verse the joining of divine determination and human guilt. The lesson the expositors draw is that the foreordination of Christ's death does not excuse the betrayer's guilt, but leaves him justly under woe. The witnesses note the first truth: "the Son of man goeth, as it was determined" — Christ's death was no accident nor mere crime of men, but the determined counsel of God, foreordained and foretold (Acts 2:23; 4:28); he went to the cross by the appointed way, in fulfilment of the divine purpose for the redemption of the world. Yet, the expositors press, this divine determination in no way lessens the guilt of the betrayer: "but woe unto that man by whom he is betrayed!" — Judas is not excused by the fact that his crime served God's purpose; the woe pronounced upon him declares his full and damnable guilt. The witnesses observe Robertson's careful statement of the balance: the determination of Christ's death "does not absolve Judas of his guilt, as the woe makes plain"; the two truths stand together — God's sovereign foreordination and man's responsible wickedness. The expositors draw the lesson on this great mystery: God overrules the sins of men to accomplish his purposes, yet the sinner is not thereby justified; Judas freely chose his treachery, acted from his own covetousness and malice, and is justly condemned, though his crime fulfilled what God had determined. The witnesses note that this is the pattern of the cross itself: Christ was "delivered by the determinate counsel and foreknowledge of God," yet taken "by wicked hands" and crucified and slain (Acts 2:23) — the divine purpose and the human guilt joined, neither cancelling the other. The expositors observe the solemnity of the "woe": better, Christ elsewhere says, that the betrayer had never been born (Matthew 26:24); his doom is fearful, his guilt unrelieved by the providence that used his crime. The witnesses draw the warning: no man may plead God's decree as excuse for his sin; the betrayer's woe stands, though his deed served the determined plan. The verse thus joins the divine determination of Christ's death with the human guilt of the betrayer — foreordained, yet damnable — teaching the mystery of God's sovereignty over man's sin, and the unrelieved guilt of the sinner whose crime fulfils the purpose of God.
23:
"And they began to inquire among themselves, which of them it was that should do this thing." The witnesses note the disciples' troubled inquiry. The Reformed Gill contrasts their response with an earlier occasion: two days before they had been struck dumb with surprise, but now "they inquired among themselves, and of Christ likewise." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "the optative is changed from the present indicative" in the indirect question, and the poignancy: "they all had their hands on the table; whose hand was it?" The witnesses note the disciples, distressed, questioning one another which of them could do such a thing.

The witnesses note the disciples' anxious self-examination: which of them could be the betrayer?

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' troubled inquiry. The lesson the expositors draw is the humble self-suspicion of the true disciples, who, hearing of a betrayer among them, searched their own hearts rather than presuming on their fidelity. The witnesses note the disciples "began to inquire among themselves" — distressed, perplexed, each asking who could do such a thing; and (as Matthew records) each asked, "Lord, is it I?" — turning the question inward, suspecting themselves rather than confidently accusing another. The expositors observe the humility this shows in the true disciples: conscious of their own weakness, they did not say "surely not I," but feared lest the betrayer be themselves; the warning of a traitor among them drove them to self-examination, not to self-confidence. The witnesses press the poignancy Robertson marks: "they all had their hands on the table; whose hand was it?" — the betrayer's hand was indistinguishable from the rest, all sharing the feast; none could tell by sight who the traitor was, and so each questioned himself and the others. The expositors draw the lesson: the announcement of sin and danger should lead the believer to humble self-searching, not to the proud assumption of his own innocence; the true disciple fears for himself, examines his own heart, and casts himself on Christ. The witnesses note the contrast with Judas, who, knowing himself the betrayer, yet (as Matthew shows) joined in the question with brazen hypocrisy — "Lord, is it I?" — adding deceit to treachery. The expositors observe the tenderness of the scene: the eleven, grieved and self-suspicious, troubled at the thought that any of them could betray their Lord; their very distress a token of their love. The witnesses draw the application: when sin is exposed or danger warned, the godly response is to ask "is it I?" — to search the heart, to fear one's own weakness, to seek Christ's keeping — not to presume on one's own faithfulness. The verse thus records the disciples' troubled inquiry — which of them could do this thing — teaching the humble self-suspicion of the true disciple, who, warned of a betrayer, examines his own heart and fears for himself rather than presuming on his fidelity.
24:
"And there was also a strife among them, which of them should be accounted the greatest." The witnesses note the unseemly dispute and its occasion. Theophylact traces it to the inquiry: "while they were inquiring who should betray the Lord, they would go on to say, You are the traitor, and so become impelled to say, I am the best, I am the greatest." The Greek Expositor suggests another cause: "the strife arose from this, that when our Lord was departing, it was thought some one must become their head." Ambrose draws the warning: "if the disciples did contend, it is not alleged as any excuse, but held out as a warning; let us beware lest contentions among us for precedence be our ruin." The witnesses note the strife over greatness, breaking out even at the last supper.

The witnesses note the jarring dispute: the disciples striving for precedence at the very table of the Lord's love.

So the witnesses make the verse the dispute over greatness. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of pride and ambition even among the disciples, at the most solemn moment, and the warning it holds against contention for precedence. The witnesses note the painful contrast: at the very supper where Christ gave his body and blood and spoke of his betrayal and death, "there was also a strife among them, which should be accounted the greatest" — the disciples, instead of being humbled by the solemnity, fell to disputing about their own rank and precedence. The expositors observe the occasions the fathers suggest: Theophylact thinks the strife grew out of the inquiry about the betrayer, the mutual suspicion turning into mutual self-assertion — "not I the traitor, but rather I the greatest"; the Greek Expositor, that with Christ departing, they disputed who should be head in his place. The witnesses press the unseemliness Ambrose marks: that the disciples contended for greatness is recorded not to excuse it but as a warning — a caution against the ambition and contention for precedence that may be the ruin of Christ's people. The expositors draw the lesson on the depth and persistence of pride: this same strife had broken out before (Luke 9:46), and now recurs at the Last Supper itself — proof that the love of preeminence is a deep-rooted sin, intruding even on the holiest occasions, even among the chosen apostles. The witnesses note the rebuke this gives to all ambition in the Church: if the apostles, in Christ's very presence, could strive for greatness, how watchful must his people be against the same spirit; and how the contention for rank and place is to be feared and shunned. The expositors observe the grace of Christ, who, instead of rebuking them harshly, answers (in the verses to come) by setting before them the example of his own humility and service. The witnesses draw the application: the disciple is to flee the strife for precedence, to count others better than himself, and to seek not greatness but lowly service. The verse thus records the dispute over greatness at the Last Supper — the disciples striving for precedence — teaching the persistence of pride, the warning against contention, and the need for the watchfulness and humility that Christ's example will commend.
25:
"And he said unto them, The kings of the Gentiles exercise lordship over them; and they that exercise authority upon them are called benefactors." The witnesses note Christ's description of worldly greatness. The grammatical-historical Barnes explains: "the kings of the nations... do this, and it is to be expected of them; our Lord does not mean that it was wrong that there should be such authority, but that his kingdom was to be of a different character." On "benefactors," Barnes and Clarke note the Greek word (euergetēs) "was applied to kings by way of compliment or flattery," and was a surname of some kings (the Ptolemies, "Euergetes"). The witnesses note Christ contrasts the worldly pattern of lordship with the way of his kingdom.

The witnesses note the foil: the Gentile kings lord it over their subjects and take flattering titles.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's description of worldly greatness as the foil to his kingdom. The lesson the expositors draw is that the greatness of the world — domineering lordship and the love of honourable titles — is the opposite of the greatness Christ commends. The witnesses note Christ's portrait of worldly rule: "the kings of the Gentiles exercise lordship over them" — the heathen princes domineer, holding power as a personal possession and lording it over their subjects; "and they that exercise authority upon them are called benefactors" — and they take to themselves flattering titles, being styled "benefactors" (a title, Barnes and Clarke note, applied to kings by way of compliment, and borne as a surname by some, like the Ptolemies). The expositors observe Barnes's careful point: Christ does not condemn the existence of such authority among the nations — civil rule is right and necessary — but sets it forth as the pattern that must not prevail in his kingdom; the greatness of the world, marked by domination and the love of honour, is not the greatness of the Church. The witnesses press the contrast Christ is drawing: the disciples had striven for precedence in the manner of the world, seeking to be greatest; and Christ holds up the worldly pattern — lordship, authority, flattering titles — precisely to disown it for his own: "But ye shall not be so" (the next verse). The expositors draw the lesson: the Church is not to be governed by the spirit of the world, where greatness means power over others and the seeking of honour; the love of preeminence, the exercise of lordship, the craving for titles, are the marks of Gentile greatness, not of Christ's kingdom. The witnesses note the rebuke to the disciples' strife: their contention for greatness was, at root, the worldly spirit, which Christ exposes by describing it; they were thinking as the Gentile kings think, not as members of his kingdom. The expositors observe the deeper irony in "benefactors": the worldly great are called benefactors for show, while truly benefiting themselves; but in Christ's kingdom, true greatness is real, lowly service. The verse thus describes worldly greatness — domineering lordship and flattering titles — as the foil to Christ's kingdom, teaching that the spirit of the world is not to rule the Church, and preparing for the great reversal Christ will declare.
26:
"But ye shall not be so: but he that is greatest among you, let him be as the younger; and he that is chief, as he that doth serve." The witnesses note the great reversal and a question about "the younger." The Reformed Gill refers to the parallel. Lightfoot reads "as the younger" of age, and observes that Peter was likely "the oldest of the whole company," which is why he had the first place in the apostolic lists and sat next the Lord. The witnesses note Christ's command: the greatest is to be as the younger (the lowest), the chief as the servant — the worldly order wholly reversed.

The witnesses note the reversal: in Christ's kingdom the greatest takes the lowest place, the chief becomes the servant.

So the witnesses make the verse the great reversal of greatness in Christ's kingdom. The lesson the expositors draw is that true greatness among Christ's people is not lordship but lowliness, not being served but serving. Christ, the witnesses note, sets his kingdom in flat opposition to the world: "But ye shall not be so" — the pattern of Gentile greatness, with its domination and titles, is forbidden to his disciples; theirs is to be the opposite. And he states the reversal: "he that is greatest among you, let him be as the younger; and he that is chief, as he that doth serve." The expositors observe the meaning: in the world, the great lord it over others and the chief are served; in Christ's kingdom, the great are to take the lowest place (as the younger, who in that culture deferred and served), and the chief are to be as servants — the way up is down, the path to greatness is humble service. The witnesses note Lightfoot's reading of "as the younger" of age, with his observation that Peter was likely the eldest of the apostles (hence his precedence in the lists); the point being that even the foremost is to behave as the youngest, the least, the servant. The expositors draw the lesson, consistent with Christ's teaching throughout (Luke 9:48; Mark 10:43-44): greatness in the Church is measured not by rank or authority but by lowliness and service; he is greatest who serves most, lowliest in his own eyes, last of all and servant of all. The witnesses press the rebuke to the disciples' strife: they had contended for precedence, seeking to be greatest in the worldly sense; Christ overturns their whole notion, teaching that the greatness they sought is found not in being above others but in serving them. The expositors observe that this is no mere counsel of modesty but the law of Christ's kingdom, grounded (as the next verse shows) in his own example. The witnesses draw the application: the disciple is to seek not greatness but service, to take the lowest place, to count it his honour to serve; and the leaders of the Church above all are to be servants, not lords, of God's people. The verse thus declares the great reversal — the greatest as the younger, the chief as the servant — teaching that true greatness in Christ's kingdom is lowly service, the worldly order wholly overturned in the way of the Lord.
27:
"For whether is greater, he that sitteth at meat, or he that serveth? is not he that sitteth at meat? but I am among you as he that serveth." The witnesses note Christ's appeal to his own example. The grammatical-historical Barnes cross-references the footwashing and Christ's self-emptying (John 13:13-14; Philippians 2:7). Ellicott draws "an obviously undesigned coincidence" with John 13: "the Lord had actually on that very evening been among them as he that serveth, girded like a slave with the linen towel, and washing the feet of the disciples." Robertson notes Christ cites his own conduct to stop their jealous contention. The witnesses note Christ, though their Lord, took the servant's place among them.

The witnesses note Christ's appeal to himself: the greater by right, yet among them as the servant.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's own example of humble service. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ enforces his teaching on greatness by his own conduct, taking the servant's place though he was Lord of all. The witnesses note the appeal to common sense: "whether is greater, he that sitteth at meat, or he that serveth? is not he that sitteth at meat?" — by the world's reckoning, the one served (the guest at table) is greater than the one who serves (the waiter); the servant is the lesser. Yet, Christ declares, "I am among you as he that serveth" — he, the Lord and Master, the greatest of all, took among them the place of the servant, the lesser; overturning by his own example the worldly order he had just described. The expositors press the force of this: Christ does not merely command humble service, but exemplifies it; he who was infinitely greater than his disciples became their servant, and so taught them that true greatness stoops to serve. The witnesses observe the striking coincidence Ellicott marks with John 13: that very evening Christ had washed the disciples' feet, girded like a slave with the towel — actually being "among them as he that serveth"; so that his words here are confirmed by the very act of that night, the Lord's hands that broke the bread also washing the disciples' feet. The expositors draw the lesson the parallel teaches: the disciples, striving for precedence, were rebuked not only by Christ's word but by his deed — the Master serving while they disputed who was greatest; the contrast could not be sharper. The witnesses note Robertson's point: Christ cites his own conduct to stop their jealous contention, the supreme example silencing their strife. The expositors observe the depth of the example: he who, being in the form of God, took the form of a servant (Philippians 2:7), is the pattern of the greatness he commends — greatness that empties itself and serves. The witnesses draw the application: the disciple is to follow his Lord, who served though he was greatest; if Christ washed feet, no service is beneath his people.
