The verse thus grounds the teaching on greatness in Christ's own example — among them as the servant — teaching that true greatness is service, enforced by the Master who, though Lord of all, took the servant's place, and that very night washed his disciples' feet.
28:
"Ye are they which have continued with me in my temptations." The witnesses note Christ's commendation of the disciples' perseverance. Theophylact draws the contrast: "as the Lord had denounced woe to the traitor, so to the rest He promises blessings, saying, Ye are they which have continued with me." Bede presses the value of perseverance: "not the first effort of patience, but long-continued perseverance, is rewarded with the glory of the heavenly kingdom; for perseverance is, so to say, the pillar and prop of all virtues." The witnesses note Christ owns the disciples as those who had stood by him through his trials.

The witnesses note the commendation: the disciples had continued with Christ in his temptations, and he owns their perseverance.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's commendation of the disciples' perseverance. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ graciously honours the perseverance of his disciples, owning their faithful continuance with him through trial. The witnesses note the tenderness of the word: having just rebuked their strife and set before them his example of service, Christ does not dwell on their faults but turns to commend them — "Ye are they which have continued with me in my temptations." The "temptations," the expositors observe, are the trials and assaults Christ endured throughout his ministry — the opposition of enemies, the hostility of the rulers, the dangers and reproaches of his earthly course; and the disciples had "continued with" him, standing by him through these, not forsaking him as many others had (John 6:66). The witnesses press the grace of the commendation: their continuance was imperfect, soon to be broken by their flight and Peter's denial; yet Christ, in his generosity, owns and honours what fidelity they had shown, crediting them with their perseverance and not upbraiding their coming failure. The expositors draw out Bede's lesson on perseverance: it is not the first effort but the long continuance that is rewarded; perseverance is the prop and pillar of all the virtues, and the disciples' continuing with Christ through his trials is the very thing he honours. The witnesses observe the contrast Theophylact marks: to the traitor, woe; to the faithful, blessing — Christ distinguishing the betrayer who fell away from the disciples who stood by him. The expositors note the encouragement this gives: Christ marks and rewards the faithful continuance of his people, however imperfect; those who stand by him through trial shall be owned and honoured by him. The witnesses draw the lesson on the value of perseverance: the disciple is called not to a momentary devotion but to a continuing faithfulness, standing by Christ through every trial; and such perseverance Christ commends and will reward, as the next verses promise. The verse thus records Christ's commendation of the disciples' perseverance — they had continued with him in his temptations — teaching the grace of Christ in honouring faithful continuance, the value of perseverance, and the distinction between the betrayer who fell and the disciples who stood by their Lord.
29:
"And I appoint unto you a kingdom, as my Father hath appointed unto me." The witnesses note the bestowal of the kingdom. The Reformed Gill takes it of "a spiritual kingdom" — the disciples' place in the government of the church as apostles, and the kingdom of grace and glory they share with all the saints. Ambrose reflects on the likeness: "the kingdom of God is not of this world... it is not equality with God, but likeness to Him, to which man must aspire; for Christ alone is the full image of God." The witnesses note Christ confers a kingdom on his disciples, as the Father had appointed one to him.

The witnesses note the gift: Christ bestowing a kingdom on his faithful disciples, as the Father bestowed one on him.

So the witnesses make the verse the bestowal of the kingdom on the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ rewards the perseverance of his disciples with a share in his own kingdom, conferred as the Father conferred it on him. The witnesses note the greatness of the gift: "I appoint unto you a kingdom" — Christ, having commended their continuance, now rewards it with a royal inheritance; he who had received a kingdom from the Father bestows a kingdom on his faithful followers. The expositors observe the pattern: "as my Father hath appointed unto me" — the kingdom Christ gives the disciples is modelled on, and flows from, the kingdom the Father gave him; they share, in their measure, in his royal dignity, as fellow-heirs with him. The witnesses press the nature of the kingdom Gill draws out: not a temporal kingdom, as the disciples had wrongly imagined, but a spiritual one — their authority in the government of the church as apostles, the kingdom of grace they share with all the saints, and the kingdom of glory to come; a royal inheritance far surpassing the worldly precedence they had striven for. The expositors draw the lesson, against the disciples' recent strife: they had contended over greatness in the worldly sense, but Christ promises them a greatness infinitely beyond it — a kingdom; let them cease their petty disputes over rank, for a royal inheritance awaits the faithful. The witnesses note Ambrose's reflection on the likeness to God: the saints are made, not equal to God, but in his likeness, as Christ is his full image; the kingdom given is a participation in the divine glory, a conformity to Christ. The expositors observe the comfort and the spur: the perseverance of the disciples, imperfect as it was, is to be crowned with a kingdom; and the promise encourages all Christ's people to continue faithful, knowing that a royal reward awaits them. The witnesses draw the application: the disciple is to set his hope not on worldly greatness but on the kingdom Christ bestows, and to persevere with Christ through trial, assured of sharing his reign. The verse thus records the bestowal of the kingdom — Christ conferring it as the Father conferred it on him — teaching the royal reward of perseverance, the disciples' share in Christ's kingdom, and the surpassing greatness that answers their faithful continuance.
30:
"That ye may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom, and sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel." The witnesses note the privileges of the kingdom — fellowship and rule. The Reformed Gill expounds the table of the kingdom: the communion of the gospel church (the Lord's table), the marriage supper of the Lamb, and the eternal feast of glory, where the saints "shall never hunger nor thirst more." On "thrones judging," Barnes cross-references the apostles' future dignity (Matthew 19:28; 1 Corinthians 6:2; Revelation 3:21). The witnesses note the two privileges: feasting at Christ's table and reigning with him in judgment.

The witnesses note the kingdom's privileges: the disciples to feast at Christ's table and to share his throne and judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the privileges of the kingdom bestowed on the disciples. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ promises his faithful disciples both the fellowship of his table and a share in his reign and judgment. The witnesses note the first privilege: "that ye may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom" — the disciples are admitted to the intimate fellowship of Christ's table; and Gill draws out its fulness — the communion of the gospel church here (the Lord's table), the marriage supper of the Lamb, and the eternal feast of glory, where the redeemed shall be satisfied with joy and hunger no more. The expositors observe the honour and joy of this: to feast at the King's table is the mark of nearest favour and richest blessing — the saints brought into the closest communion with their Lord, partakers of his joy. The second privilege, the witnesses note, is rule: "and sit on thrones judging the twelve tribes of Israel" — the disciples to share in Christ's royal authority and judgment; Barnes refers it to the apostles' future dignity (Matthew 19:28), their place of honour and rule in the kingdom, and the saints' share in the judgment (1 Corinthians 6:2). The expositors press the greatness of the reward against the disciples' strife: they had contended for precedence, and Christ promises them thrones — not the petty rank they sought, but a share in his own reign and judgment; the faithful are to be kings and judges with Christ. The witnesses observe the meaning of "judging the twelve tribes": the apostles, who had been despised by the rulers of Israel, would be exalted to judge them; and, more broadly, the saints shall share in Christ's judicial glory at the last. The expositors draw the lesson: the rewards of the kingdom — fellowship at the table, thrones of judgment — infinitely surpass all the disciples had striven for; let them cease their disputes over worldly greatness, for a royal and eternal honour awaits the faithful. The witnesses note the comfort: those who continue with Christ through trial shall feast with him and reign with him; the perseverance of the disciples is crowned with the highest privileges of the kingdom. The verse thus records the privileges of the kingdom — feasting at Christ's table and reigning on thrones — teaching the surpassing reward of perseverance, the fellowship and the rule promised to the faithful, and the royal inheritance that answers their continuance with Christ.
31:
"And the Lord said, Simon, Simon, behold, Satan hath desired to have you, that he may sift you as wheat." The witnesses note Satan's assault and its divine permission. Bede sets it against the disciples' boast: "lest the eleven should be boastful and impute it to their own strength that they almost alone continued with our Lord, He shows them that, if not protected by their Master, they would have been beaten down as the rest... Satan hath desired to sift you as wheat — to tempt and shake you, as he who cleanses wheat by winnowing; wherein He teaches that no man's faith is tried unless God permits it." Theophylact notes it was said to Peter "because he was bolder than the rest, and might feel proud because of the things Christ had promised." Cyril reads it as a warning against self-exaltation. The witnesses note the doubled name "Simon, Simon" — an earnest, tender warning.

The witnesses note Satan's desire to sift the disciples, permitted by God as a trial.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning of Satan's assault. The lesson the expositors draw is that Satan seeks to sift and shake the disciples, but only as God permits, and that this should humble, not exalt, the believer. The witnesses note the earnest, tender address — "Simon, Simon" — the doubled name marking the urgency and affection of Christ's warning to the disciple most in danger. The "sifting as wheat," the expositors observe (with Bede), is the image of the winnower who shakes the wheat to separate it: Satan desires to tempt and shake the disciples violently, to test their faith and, if he can, to scatter it as chaff. The witnesses press the divine permission Bede draws out: "no man's faith is tried unless God permits it" — Satan can do nothing against the disciples but by leave; his desire to sift them is bounded by God's permission, as in the case of Job. The expositors draw the humbling lesson Bede names: this is said lest the disciples, who had continued with Christ and been promised a kingdom, should grow proud and impute their steadfastness to their own strength; Christ shows them that, left to themselves, they would be beaten down like the rest, and that their standing is wholly by his protection. The witnesses note Theophylact's point that the warning was aimed especially at Peter, the boldest, who might be lifted up by Christ's promises and most needed the caution. The expositors observe the realism of the warning: the same disciples just commended for perseverance are warned that Satan seeks their ruin — their fidelity is not their own achievement but God's gift, and their security lies not in their strength but in Christ's keeping. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is not to presume on his own steadfastness, for Satan desires to sift him; his safety is in the permission of God who bounds the tempter, and in the intercession of Christ (the next verse). The verse thus records Satan's desire to sift the disciples — permitted by God — teaching the reality of the tempter's assault, its limitation by divine permission, and the humbling truth that the believer stands not by his own strength but by Christ's protection.
32:
"But I have prayed for thee, that thy faith fail not: and when thou art converted, strengthen thy brethren." The witnesses note Christ's intercession for Peter and the charge to him. The Reformed Gill notes Christ "prayed for all the apostles, but particularly for Peter, because he was in the greatest danger." Lightfoot marks the emphasis on "faith": the other apostles' courage failed and they fled (Mark 14:50), but "their faith was nothing so near shipwreck as Peter's faith was" when he renounced and abjured his Lord; yet Christ's prayer kept Peter's faith from utter failure. The witnesses note the threefold word: Christ's prayer for Peter, the keeping of his faith, and the charge to strengthen the brethren when restored.

The witnesses note Christ's intercession: he prayed for Peter that his faith should not finally fail, and charged him, once restored, to strengthen others.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's intercession for Peter and the charge that follows. The lesson the expositors draw is the saving power of Christ's intercession, which keeps the faith of his own from final failure, and the use the restored sinner is to make of his recovery. The witnesses note the gracious "But" — against Satan's desire to sift Peter, Christ sets his own prayer: "I have prayed for thee, that thy faith fail not." The expositors observe the contrast that secures Peter: Satan desired to have him, but Christ interceded for him; and the intercession of Christ prevailed over the malice of Satan, so that Peter, though he would fall grievously, would not finally perish — his faith, though shaken, would not utterly fail. The witnesses press Lightfoot's note on the emphasis on "faith": Peter's denial was a dreadful fall, his courage utterly failing, his lips abjuring his Lord; yet his faith, by Christ's prayer, was not destroyed — the root remained, and he repented and was restored, where Judas, unprayed-for, perished. The expositors draw the great lesson on Christ's intercession: the perseverance of the believer rests not on his own strength (which fails, as Peter's did) but on the prayer of Christ his High Priest, who ever lives to make intercession (Hebrews 7:25); it was Christ's prayer that kept Peter from the fate of Judas, and it is Christ's intercession that keeps every believer from final apostasy. The witnesses note the tender foreknowledge: Christ knew Peter would fall ("when thou art converted" — when turned back), yet prayed not that he should not fall but that his faith should not fail — providing for his recovery before his fall. The expositors observe the charge that follows: "when thou art converted, strengthen thy brethren" — Peter, restored from his fall, is to use his recovery to confirm and encourage others; the experience of his own weakness and Christ's restoring grace fitting him to strengthen the weak. The witnesses draw the lesson: the restored sinner, humbled by his fall and renewed by grace, is to comfort and stablish his brethren, turning his recovery to their good. The verse thus records Christ's intercession for Peter — keeping his faith from final failure — and the charge to strengthen the brethren, teaching the saving power of Christ's prayer, the perseverance of faith by his intercession, and the ministry of the restored to the weak.
33:
"And he said unto him, Lord, I am ready to go with thee, both into prison, and to death." The witnesses note Peter's confident boast. The grammatical-historical Ellicott detects a "latent tone of indignation as well as devotion": Peter "half-resented the thought that a special prayer should be necessary for him." Robertson agrees: "evidently Peter was not flattered by the need of Christ's earnest prayers for his welfare and loyalty; hence this loud boast." The witnesses note Peter's self-confident profession — ready, as he thought, for prison and death — which his coming denial would expose.

The witnesses note Peter's overconfident boast: protesting his readiness for prison and death, even as his fall drew near.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's self-confident boast. The lesson the expositors draw is the peril of self-confidence, which presumes on its own strength and goes before a fall. The witnesses note Peter's profession: "Lord, I am ready to go with thee, both into prison, and to death" — a bold avowal of fidelity, expressing real love but resting on a false confidence in his own steadfastness. The expositors observe the tone the moderns detect: Ellicott and Robertson find in Peter's words a touch of indignation as well as devotion — Peter half-resented that Christ should think a special prayer necessary for him, and answered with this loud boast, as if to say his loyalty needed no such safeguard. The witnesses press the self-confidence this betrays: Peter trusted in his own resolve, his own love, his own courage; he did not know his own weakness, nor that, left to himself, he would fall. The expositors draw the lesson: self-confidence is the prelude to failure; the believer who presumes on his own strength, who boasts of what he will do and cannot conceive of falling, is in the greatest danger — for "let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall" (1 Corinthians 10:12). The witnesses note the contrast with Christ's warning: Christ had just told Peter that Satan desired to sift him and that his faith needed Christ's prayer to keep it; Peter, instead of trembling and seeking that prayer, boasts of his readiness — meeting the warning with self-assurance rather than humble dependence. The expositors observe the tragedy soon to follow: this very Peter, so confident of going to prison and death, would within hours deny his Lord thrice before a servant-girl; the boast exposed by the fall. The witnesses draw the application: the disciple is not to trust his own strength nor boast of his fidelity, but to fear his own weakness and cast himself on Christ's keeping; self-confidence is no virtue but a snare. The verse thus records Peter's overconfident boast — ready, as he thought, for prison and death — teaching the peril of self-confidence, the believer's ignorance of his own weakness, and the lesson that he who presumes on his own steadfastness is nearest to a fall.
34:
"And he said, I tell thee, Peter, the cock shall not crow this day, before that thou shalt thrice deny that thou knowest me." The witnesses note the precise prediction of Peter's threefold denial. The grammatical-historical Clarke harmonizes the "day"/"night" of the accounts: "the Jewish day, of twenty-four hours, began with the evening"; so "this day" (Luke) and "this night" (Matthew, Mark) agree. Robertson notes "thrice" is in all four Gospels, and that Peter would "even deny knowing Jesus." The witnesses note the exactness of the prediction — before the cock crows that day, Peter would thrice deny his Lord.

The witnesses note the precise foretelling: Peter's threefold denial before the cock-crow, answering his boast.

So the witnesses make the verse the prediction of Peter's denial. The lesson the expositors draw is the precise foreknowledge of Christ, who foretells Peter's fall to the very detail, answering his boast with a humbling truth. The witnesses note the solemn directness — "I tell thee, Peter" — Christ naming the disciple and stating plainly what would come: "the cock shall not crow this day, before that thou shalt thrice deny that thou knowest me." The expositors press the exactness of the prediction: not merely that Peter would fail, but the precise extent (thrice), the precise depth (denying that he even knew Christ), and the precise time (before the cock-crow that very day) — a foreknowledge that only the omniscient Lord could possess, foretelling the secret future failure of his disciple to the detail. The witnesses observe Clarke's harmonizing of the time: Luke's "this day" and the "this night" of Matthew and Mark agree, the Jewish day beginning at evening; so the denial would come that very night, before the cock crew. The expositors draw the lesson on the contrast with Peter's boast: Peter had just professed his readiness for prison and death; Christ answers with the humbling truth that, far from dying for him, Peter would deny even knowing him, and that within hours — the proud boast met by the precise prophecy of the fall. The witnesses note the depth of the denial foretold: not a mere wavering, but a threefold, emphatic disowning — "that thou knowest me" — the disciple denying acquaintance with his Lord; the deepest fall, foretold with sorrowful exactness. The expositors observe the grace hidden in the prediction: by foretelling the denial, Christ both humbles Peter's self-confidence and prepares his recovery — for the remembrance of this word (which the crowing cock would bring) would break Peter's heart to repentance (Luke 22:61-62). The witnesses draw the application: the precise fulfilment of so detailed a prophecy confirms the deity of Christ and the certainty of his word; and the foretelling of Peter's fall warns every disciple against self-confidence and assures him of Christ's foreknowing care. The verse thus records the prediction of Peter's threefold denial — before the cock-crow that day — teaching Christ's exact foreknowledge, the humbling of Peter's boast, and the grace that foretells the fall to prepare the recovery.
35:
"And he said unto them, When I sent you without purse, and scrip, and shoes, lacked ye any thing? And they said, Nothing." The witnesses note the recollection of the disciples' first mission. Cyril sets the transition: having foretold Peter's denial at his being taken, Christ "next announces the struggle that would ensue against the Jews" — recalling how he had sent the apostles "to take no thought of the things of the body, but to place their whole hope of salvation in him." Chrysostom draws the image of the swimming-teacher: "as one who teaches to swim at first supports his pupils with his hands, but afterward withdraws his hand and bids them help themselves; so Christ dealt with His disciples — at the beginning He was present, giving abundance of all things... but when it was necessary for them to show their own strength, He withdrew for a little His grace." The witnesses note the disciples' answer: on their first mission, sent without provision, they lacked nothing.

The witnesses note Christ's reminder: when first sent without provision, the disciples lacked nothing, divine care supplying all.

So the witnesses make the verse the recollection of the first mission's provision. The lesson the expositors draw is the contrast between the sheltered days of Christ's visible care and the harder times now coming, to prepare the disciples for the change. The witnesses note Christ's question, recalling the first sending (Luke 9:3; 10:4): "When I sent you without purse, and scrip, and shoes, lacked ye any thing?" — and the disciples answer, "Nothing"; for in those early days, sent out without provision, they had wanted for nothing, the providence of God and the favour of the people supplying all their need. The expositors observe the purpose of the reminder: Christ is drawing a contrast, preparing the disciples for the changed and harder conditions now coming (the next verse) — the time of persecution and hostility, when they would no longer be welcomed but hunted. Cyril notes the transition: having foretold his being taken, Christ announces the struggle that would follow against the Jews and the world. The witnesses press Chrysostom's beautiful image of the swimming-teacher: as one teaching a child to swim first supports him with his hands, then withdraws them and bids him help himself, even letting him sink a little; so Christ, who at the first had been present with his disciples, supplying them richly, would now withdraw for a season his visible care, that they might exercise their own faith and strength — not abandoning them, but teaching them to depend on him in harder ways. The expositors draw the lesson: the disciple's experience changes; there are sheltered days of evident provision and favour, and harder days of trial and want; and Christ prepares his people for the change, that they may not be dismayed when the easier season gives way to the harder. The witnesses note the comfort in the recollection: the God who supplied all their need in the first mission is the same who would keep them in the trials to come, though by a harder discipline. The verse thus records the recollection of the first mission's provision — they lacked nothing — teaching the contrast with the coming days of hardship, Christ's preparing of his disciples for the change, and the abiding care that, like the swimming-teacher, withdraws its visible support only to strengthen their faith.
36:
"Then said he unto them, But now, he that hath a purse, let him take it, and likewise his scrip: and he that hath no sword, let him sell his garment, and buy one." The witnesses note the changed conditions and the much-debated "sword." Albert Barnes reads the literal sense of the change: "the Saviour says the times are changed; before, he sent them out... in their own country, among friends; now they were to go among strangers and to encounter dangers." Lightfoot insists the "sword" is not meant literally: "doth our Saviour give them this counsel in good earnest? He uses the common dialect... He warns them of a danger very near, and in a common way of speech lets them know that they had more need of providing swords for their defence than be quarrelling amongst themselves — not so much exhorting them to repel force with force, as to give them such an apprehension of the common rage of their enemies that might suppress all private animosities." The witnesses note the proverbial warning of coming hostility.

The witnesses note the changed conditions: the disciples now to expect hardship and hostility, the "sword" a figure of the coming danger.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning of changed and hostile times. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ forewarns his disciples of the coming hardship and opposition, the "sword" being a proverbial figure for the danger they must face, not a literal call to arms. The witnesses note the contrast with the first mission: "But now" — the times are changed; where before the disciples went among friends and lacked nothing, now they must provide for themselves and prepare to meet hostility; the sheltered days are over, and harder times begin. The expositors observe Barnes's reading of the literal change: the disciples, formerly sent among their own people who received them, would now go among strangers and enemies, facing dangers and want; hence they must take purse and scrip, making provision as those entering a hostile world. The great question, the witnesses press, is the "sword": "he that hath no sword, let him sell his garment, and buy one." The expositors note Lightfoot's strong reading, that this is not meant literally — Christ does not counsel his disciples to arm for violence (as his rebuke of Peter's sword in Gethsemane, and "all they that take the sword shall perish," Matthew 26:52, make plain); rather, he uses a common proverbial expression to warn them vividly of the danger and hostility now near, that they had more need to think of defence against the common enemy than to quarrel among themselves over precedence. The witnesses observe the purpose Lightfoot draws: the figure of the sword impresses on the disciples the gravity of the coming opposition, suppressing their petty rivalries by setting before them the real and pressing danger they would all share. The expositors draw the lesson on the changed conditions of discipleship: the disciple must expect, in this world, not ease and favour but hardship, opposition, and the need for endurance; Christ forewarns his people that the way ahead is hard. The witnesses note the misunderstanding the disciples fell into (the next verse, not in this block: "Lord, here are two swords"), taking the figure literally — which Christ would correct. The verse thus records the warning of changed and hostile times — purse, scrip, and the figurative "sword" — teaching that Christ prepares his disciples for the coming hardship and opposition, the sword a vivid figure of the danger, not a call to arms, and the harder discipline of discipleship in a hostile world.
37:
"For I say unto you, that this that is written must yet be accomplished in me, And he was reckoned among the transgressors: for the things concerning me have an end." The witnesses note the fulfilment of prophecy in Christ's being numbered with transgressors. The grammatical-historical Barnes cites the source: "Isaiah 53:12 — was reckoned among the transgressors; not reckoned as a transgressor, but among or with them — he was treated as transgressors are, put to death in their company"; yet "he was innocent, holy, harmless, and undefiled" (Hebrews 7:26). Lightfoot reads "the things concerning me have an end" as Christ's word that his earthly work draws to its close: "My business is done, yours is but beginning." The witnesses note Christ applies Isaiah's prophecy of the suffering Servant to himself.

The witnesses note the prophecy fulfilled: the innocent Christ to be numbered with transgressors, his earthly course coming to its end.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's declaration that he must be numbered with transgressors in fulfilment of prophecy. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ's suffering and shameful death were foretold, and that his being treated as a criminal was the appointed fulfilment of the word concerning him. The witnesses note Christ's solemn "must" — "this that is written must yet be accomplished in me" — marking the divine necessity: the prophecy of Isaiah 53:12, that the Servant should be "numbered with the transgressors," must be fulfilled in him; his death was no accident but the accomplishment of Scripture. The expositors press the meaning Barnes draws: Christ was reckoned "among the transgressors" — not that he was a transgressor, for he was sinless, but that he was treated as one, condemned as a criminal, crucified between two thieves, put to death in their company; the spotless Lamb numbered with the guilty. The witnesses observe the depth of this humiliation: the Holy One of God, innocent and undefiled, was reckoned with malefactors, bearing in his death the shame of the worst — a fulfilment both of the prophecy and of the truth that he bore the sins of many. The expositors draw the lesson on the substitution: he was numbered with transgressors because he stood in the transgressors' place, bearing their sin and their shame, that they might be reckoned righteous in him. The witnesses note Lightfoot's reading of "the things concerning me have an end": Christ's earthly work was drawing to its close, his course of suffering about to be finished; and the saying carries a tender note to the disciples — his business is ending, theirs beginning, and this is no time for them to contend. The verse thus records Christ's declaration that he must be numbered with transgressors in fulfilment of Isaiah — the innocent treated as guilty, the prophecy accomplished — teaching the divine necessity of his shameful death, his substitution in the place of sinners, and the drawing to its end of his appointed course.
38:
"And they said, Lord, behold, here are two swords. And he said unto them, It is enough." The witnesses note the disciples' literal misunderstanding and Christ's reply. The Reformed Gill notes "they understood Christ's words literally; and two swords being among them" — brought from Galilee for defence against robbers on the roads — "one of these, as appears afterwards, belonged to Peter." Barnes observes the Galileans often went armed because the country was "full of robbers and wild beasts." On "It is enough," Barnes admits the difficulty; the witnesses note it is best read not as "two swords suffice" (for arms) but as a way of closing the misunderstood conversation — enough of this.

The witnesses note the disciples' carnal misreading of the "sword," and Christ's brief, closing reply.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' misunderstanding of the sword. The lesson the expositors draw is the dullness of the disciples in taking Christ's figurative warning literally, and the gentle way Christ closes the misunderstanding. The witnesses note the disciples' response: "Lord, behold, here are two swords" — taking Christ's word about buying a sword (v.36) in the bare literal sense, they point to the two swords already among them (brought, Gill notes, for defence against the robbers of the Galilean roads, one being Peter's). The expositors observe the carnal misreading: Christ had used the sword as a figure of the coming danger and hostility, warning them of the hard times ahead; but the disciples, dull as ever to his figurative speech, thought he meant literal weapons, and produced their two swords. And Christ's reply, "It is enough," the witnesses press, is best understood not as approval (as though two swords were a sufficient armoury) — for two swords could never defend against the world, and Christ would shortly rebuke Peter's use of one — but as a way of breaking off the misunderstood conversation: enough of this, no more of such talk; the disciples having missed his meaning, Christ does not labour to correct them then, but ends the matter. The expositors draw the lesson on the disciples' dullness: how slow they were to grasp the spiritual meaning of Christ's words, how prone to the literal and carnal; and how patient Christ was with their misunderstanding, neither sharply rebuking nor pursuing the point. The witnesses note the irony the night would expose: the disciples thought two swords enough for the crisis, but the crisis was not one for swords at all; and Peter's drawing of the sword would only prove how far they had mistaken their Master's meaning. The expositors observe that the verse prepares for the rebuke of the sword in Gethsemane, where Christ would heal the wound Peter's misunderstanding inflicted. The verse thus records the disciples' literal misreading of the sword and Christ's closing "It is enough" — teaching their dullness to his figurative warning, the patience of Christ in breaking off the misunderstanding, and the prelude to the rebuke of carnal weapons that the arrest would bring.
39:
"And he came out, and went, as he was wont, to the mount of Olives; and his disciples also followed him." The witnesses note Christ's habitual retreat and his willing approach to his Passion. Bede observes the providence and the will: "as He was to be betrayed by His disciple, our Lord goes to the place of His wonted retirement, where He might most easily be found" — not avoiding the betrayer but going where Judas knew to seek him. Dionysius of Alexandria and Bede (on the cup) note the deliberate approach to suffering. The witnesses note Christ went "as he was wont" — his customary place of prayer — followed by his disciples.

The witnesses note Christ's habitual retreat to Olivet, where, knowing the betrayer would seek him, he willingly went to meet his Passion.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's willing approach to Gethsemane. The lesson the expositors draw is the voluntariness of Christ's suffering, displayed in his going to the very place where the betrayer would find him. The witnesses note that Christ went "as he was wont, to the mount of Olives" — his customary place of retirement and prayer, where he often withdrew with his disciples. The expositors press the significance Bede draws out: Christ, knowing he was to be betrayed, went to the very place his betrayer knew — not fleeing, not hiding, but going openly to where Judas could most easily find him; the Shepherd advancing willingly to meet the wolf. This, the witnesses observe, is the mark of his voluntary Passion: Christ did not wait to be hunted down, nor seek to escape, but went of his own accord to the appointed place, offering himself to the arrest he foreknew. The expositors draw the lesson: Christ laid down his life of himself, no man taking it from him (John 10:18); his going to Olivet, his usual haunt, where the betrayer would seek him, is the proof of his willing self-surrender — he met his Passion, not by compulsion, but by choice. The witnesses note the disciples following: they accompanied him, not knowing what the night would bring, into the garden of his agony. The expositors observe the tender familiarity of the place: Olivet, where Christ had so often prayed, would now be the scene of his deepest sorrow — the garden of his agony hard by the city of his coming death. The witnesses draw the application: Christ's willing approach to his Passion is the pattern and the ground of the believer's salvation — a Saviour who went freely to the cross, meeting his betrayer, offering himself for his people; and a comfort that his sufferings were no accident but his own deliberate, loving act. The verse thus records Christ's habitual retreat to Olivet, where he willingly went to meet his Passion — teaching the voluntariness of his suffering, his offering of himself to the betrayer, and the willing self-surrender by which he laid down his life for his own.
40:
"And when he was at the place, he said unto them, Pray that ye enter not into temptation." The witnesses note Christ's charge to pray against temptation. The grammatical-historical Robertson presses the force: "Keep on praying not to enter (ingressive aorist, not even once) into temptation; it is real temptation here, not just trial; Jesus knew the power of temptation and the need of prayer" — and these words "throw light on" the petition of the Lord's Prayer (Matthew 6:13). The witnesses note Christ, on entering the garden, bids the disciples pray that they be not overcome in the coming trial.

The witnesses note Christ's first word in the garden: a charge to the disciples to pray against entering temptation.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's charge to pray against temptation. The lesson the expositors draw is that prayer is the appointed safeguard against being overcome in temptation, a charge Christ gave his disciples on the eve of their great trial. The witnesses note that Christ, on reaching the place of his agony, turned first to his disciples with a word for their good: "Pray that ye enter not into temptation." The expositors press Robertson's careful reading: the charge is to "keep on praying" not to "enter into" temptation — not merely to be tried (trial being unavoidable), but to be overcome, to fall into the snare; Christ would have them pray to be kept from yielding when the testing came. The witnesses observe the fitness of the warning: the disciples stood on the brink of the greatest trial of their faith — the arrest, the scattering, Peter's denial; and Christ, knowing the power of the coming temptation and their weakness, charges them to pray, that they might be kept. The expositors draw the lesson on prayer as the safeguard: the believer cannot avoid temptation, but he may, by prayer, be kept from being overcome by it; the way to stand in the hour of trial is to pray beforehand for keeping, as Christ here teaches. The witnesses note Robertson's link to the Lord's Prayer: this charge illuminates the petition "lead us not into temptation" (Matthew 6:13) — the prayer to be kept from falling in the time of testing, which Christ here applies to his disciples' immediate crisis. The expositors observe the tragedy that followed: the disciples did not heed the charge but slept, and so, unprepared by prayer, were overcome — fleeing and (Peter) denying; their failure the proof that the unprayed-for soul falls in temptation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to watch and pray against temptation, especially in seasons of approaching trial; for prayer is the appointed means of keeping, and the neglect of it leaves the soul exposed to the snare. The verse thus records Christ's charge to pray against temptation — teaching prayer as the safeguard of the soul in trial, the need to pray beforehand for keeping, and the warning that the unprayed-for, like the sleeping disciples, are overcome when the hour of temptation comes.
41:
"And he was withdrawn from them about a stone's cast, and kneeled down, and prayed." The witnesses note the eyewitness detail and the posture of prayer. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks "the descriptive touch, implying a report coming directly or indirectly from an eye-witness, is peculiar to St. Luke," and notes the tense — "was praying" — implying "continuous and sustained prayer." Robertson harmonizes the postures: Luke's "kneeled down," Mark's "fell on the ground," Matthew's "fell on his face" — "all could be true at different moments." The witnesses note Christ withdrew a little from the disciples to pray alone.

The witnesses note Christ's withdrawal a stone's cast to pray, kneeling in sustained prayer.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's withdrawal to pray. The lesson the expositors draw is the earnestness and solitude of Christ's prayer in his agony, and the eyewitness vividness of Luke's account. The witnesses note the detail "about a stone's cast" — the precise, descriptive touch that Ellicott marks as peculiar to Luke, implying the report of an eyewitness (perhaps the three disciples who watched, or the tradition Luke gathered); a small token of the historical truth of the narrative. The expositors observe the posture: Christ "kneeled down, and prayed" — and Robertson harmonizes this with the other Gospels' "fell on the ground" and "fell on his face," noting that all could be true at different moments of the agony, as Christ knelt, then fell prostrate, in the intensity of his prayer. The witnesses press the earnestness Ellicott draws from the tense: "was praying" — continuous, sustained prayer, not a brief petition but a prolonged wrestling with the Father in his agony. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's solitude in suffering: he withdrew from even the three trusted disciples, a little apart, to pour out his soul alone before the Father; the deepest sorrows are borne in solitude with God, and Christ, in his agony, sought the Father's face alone. The witnesses note the example for the believer: in the hour of distress, to withdraw and pray, kneeling before God, pouring out the soul in sustained and earnest prayer, as Christ did. The expositors observe the tenderness of the scene: the Son of God on his knees, prostrate, praying with all earnestness in the garden of his agony — the eternal Word, made man, wrestling in prayer as the cross drew near. The verse thus records Christ's withdrawal to pray — a stone's cast apart, kneeling in sustained prayer — teaching the earnestness and solitude of his prayer in his agony, the eyewitness truth of Luke's account, and the example of fervent, solitary prayer in the hour of deepest need.
42:
"Saying, Father, if thou be willing, remove this cup from me: nevertheless not my will, but thine, be done." The witnesses note the prayer of submission. The Reformed Gill explains "if thou be willing" as "if it be consistent with thy will of saving sinners." Ellicott hears "a more distinct echo of the prayer which He had taught His disciples" — Christ too could say "Thy will be done," and his petition was "subject... to the antecedent condition that it was in harmony with Thy will." Robertson notes "be done" (present middle imperative) — "keep on being done, the Father's will." The witnesses note Christ's prayer that the cup might pass, yet wholly submitted to the Father's will.

The witnesses note the great prayer of submission: the cup feared, yet the Father's will wholly embraced.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's prayer of submission in the garden. The lesson the expositors draw is the perfect submission of Christ to the Father's will, even in the agony of shrinking from the cup. The witnesses note the two parts of the prayer. First, the petition: "Father, if thou be willing, remove this cup from me" — the cup being the suffering of the cross, the bearing of sin and the wrath due to it; and Christ, in his true human nature, shrinks from it, praying that, if it be consistent with the Father's will of saving sinners, it might pass. The expositors observe that this shrinking is no sinful reluctance, but the natural recoil of his sinless humanity from the dreadful cup — the bearing of the world's sin and the hiding of the Father's face; he prays as truly man, feeling the horror of what lay before him. But the second part, the witnesses press, governs all: "nevertheless not my will, but thine, be done" — the complete and absolute submission of his human will to the Father's; whatever his human nature shrank from, he yields wholly to the Father's purpose. The expositors draw the lesson on the submission: Christ's prayer is the pattern of all true prayer and all true obedience — to bring our desires to the Father, yet to subject them entirely to his will; to ask, but always "if thou be willing," and to embrace whatever he wills. Ellicott's note, the witnesses observe, draws the echo of the Lord's Prayer: Christ himself prays "Thy will be done," teaching his disciples by his own example to make every petition subject to the Father's will. The expositors note the cost of this submission: it was no easy resignation, but a yielding in the midst of agony, the human will surrendering to the Father's though it cost the cross. The witnesses draw the comfort and the example: Christ's submission won our salvation, for by his "not my will but thine" he embraced the cross for us; and his prayer is the model for every believer in trial — to submit, in the deepest distress, to the will of God. The verse thus records Christ's prayer of submission — the cup feared, the Father's will embraced — teaching the perfect obedience of Christ, the pattern of submissive prayer, and the costly yielding by which he accepted the cross for our redemption.
43:
"And there appeared an angel unto him from heaven, strengthening him." The witnesses note the comforting angel and the two natures of Christ. Theophylact draws the lesson: "to make known to us the power of prayer, that we may exercise it in adversity, our Lord when praying is comforted by an angel." Bede reads it as a testimony to both natures: "in testimony of each nature, angels are said both to have ministered to Him and comforted Him; for the Creator needed not the protection of His creature, but being made man, as for our sakes He is sad, so for our sakes He is comforted." Ambrose guards against misreading the agony as weakness, holding it taken upon him for us. (The witnesses note verses 43-44 are absent from some early manuscripts, though widely attested.)

The witnesses note the angel strengthening Christ — a comfort to his human nature in the agony.

So the witnesses make the verse the strengthening of Christ by the angel. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, in his true humanity, was comforted by an angel, displaying the reality of his manhood and the power of prayer answered. The witnesses note the wonder: "there appeared an angel unto him from heaven, strengthening him" — the Creator strengthened by a creature, the Lord of angels comforted by one of his own ministering spirits. The expositors press the resolution Bede gives: this is no contradiction of Christ's deity, but a testimony to his two natures — as God, he needed no creature's help; but as man, made truly human for our sakes, he was strengthened, even as for our sakes he was sorrowful. The angel's ministry, the witnesses observe, answers to the reality of Christ's manhood: he who took our nature took also its weakness (sin excepted), and was comforted in his agony as a man, that the truth of his incarnation might be confirmed. The expositors draw the lesson Theophylact names: Christ's being comforted in prayer teaches the power of prayer answered in adversity — that the soul which prays earnestly in distress shall be strengthened, as Christ was; his example commends fervent prayer in suffering, and assures us that God sends help to those who pray. The witnesses note Ambrose's guard against the heretical misreading: the agony and the need of comfort are not signs of inherent weakness in Christ, nor of a defective deity, but of the real human nature he took upon him for our salvation — he was sorrowful and strengthened for us, in the manhood by which he redeemed us. The expositors observe the textual question: verses 43-44 are absent from some ancient manuscripts, yet widely attested and received by the Church as genuine, recording the angelic comfort and the bloody sweat. The witnesses draw the comfort: that Christ, in his deepest agony, was not left alone but strengthened from heaven — a pledge that God strengthens his suffering people, and that the prayers of the afflicted are answered with help. The verse thus records the strengthening of Christ by the angel — the Creator comforted as man — teaching the reality of his humanity, the answering of prayer in adversity, and the truth that his agony was borne, and his comfort received, for our sakes.
44:
"And being in an agony he prayed more earnestly: and his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down to the ground." The witnesses note the agony, the bloody sweat, and its cause. Chrysostom reads it as proof of the real incarnation: "because not in appearance but in reality He took upon Himself our flesh, in order to confirm the truth of the dispensation, He submits to bear human suffering." The Reformed Gill weighs the cause: an "agony" or conflict — with Satan, who "now appeared visibly to him," and above all with the prospect of bearing sin and the wrath due to it; not death merely, but death "as it was to be inflicted" as the punishment of sin. Clarke notes much has been written "to little purpose" on the cause, mentioning (without endorsing) the view that "the Divine wrath" pressed on him, God treating him "as if he were a sinner." The witnesses note the bloody sweat — a token of the extremity of the agony.

The witnesses note the depth of the agony: Christ praying more earnestly, his sweat as drops of blood.

So the witnesses make the verse the agony and bloody sweat of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the reality and the depth of Christ's suffering in the garden, and the debated cause of so dreadful an agony. The witnesses note the intensity: "being in an agony he prayed more earnestly" — the agony, the conflict, deepening his prayer, so that he wrestled with the Father with ever greater fervour; and "his sweat was as it were great drops of blood falling down to the ground" — the bloody sweat, a rare and extreme token of anguish, the very blood forced through the pores by the violence of his distress. The expositors press Chrysostom's point: this proves the reality of the incarnation — Christ took real flesh, and bore real human suffering, not in appearance but in truth; the bloody sweat is the seal of his genuine humanity and genuine agony. The witnesses observe the debated cause, on which the expositors differ. Gill names two: a conflict with Satan, who assaulted him in the garden (the prince of this world coming to him, John 14:30); and, more deeply, the prospect of bearing the sin of the world and the wrath due to it — not death merely, but death as the punishment of sin, the cup of divine wrath. Clarke notes (without fully endorsing) the view that the Father's wrath pressed upon him, God treating the sin-bearer as a sinner; and the witnesses observe that the deepest cause lay here — in the anticipation of the cross as the bearing of sin and the hiding of the Father's face, the soul of Christ overwhelmed at what he was about to endure for us. The expositors draw the lesson on the cost of redemption: the bloody sweat of Gethsemane shows what our salvation cost the Saviour — an agony of soul before the agony of body, the dreadful weight of sin and wrath pressing on him even in the garden. The witnesses draw the comfort and the wonder: that Christ endured such agony for our sakes, sweating blood in the garden before shedding it on the cross; the measure of his love seen in the measure of his suffering. The verse thus records the agony and bloody sweat of Christ — praying more earnestly in his extremity — teaching the reality and depth of his suffering, the debated but dreadful cause in the bearing of sin and wrath, and the costly love that endured such agony for our redemption.
45:
"And when he rose up from prayer, and was come to his disciples, he found them sleeping for sorrow." The witnesses note the disciples asleep and Luke's psychological insight. Ellicott marks it "characteristic of St. Luke, that while the other Gospels state simply that the disciples slept, he assigns it psychologically and physiologically to its cause" — "prolonged sorrow has at last a numbing and narcotising effect" (cf. "believing not for joy," Luke 24:41). Robertson notes Luke does not tell of "the three turnings of Jesus to the trusted three for human sympathy." The witnesses note the disciples overcome with sleep, their sorrow exhausting them.

The witnesses note the disciples found sleeping — overcome by sorrow's numbing effect — while Christ prayed in agony.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples asleep for sorrow. The lesson the expositors draw is the weakness of the disciples even in their love, and the tender insight of Luke in tracing their sleep to its cause. The witnesses note the contrast: while Christ wrestled in agony and bloody sweat, the disciples, whom he had charged to watch and pray, "found them sleeping" — overcome by drowsiness at the very hour of their Master's deepest need. The expositors observe the distinctive touch Ellicott marks: Luke alone adds "for sorrow" — assigning the disciples' sleep not to mere indifference or carelessness, but to the numbing, narcotizing effect of prolonged grief; their sorrow at the things Christ had foretold (his betrayal, death, departure) had worn them out, and exhausted nature gave way to sleep. The witnesses press the tenderness of this: Luke, the physician-evangelist, with his eye for the workings of the heart, does not harshly condemn the disciples but explains their failure — their sleep the effect of overwhelming sorrow, the same sorrow that would later keep them from believing the resurrection "for joy" (Luke 24:41). The expositors draw the lesson, charitably yet seriously: the disciples' love was real, but their flesh was weak; sorrow overcame them where they should have watched and prayed, and their sleep left them unprepared for the trial that was upon them. The witnesses note Robertson's observation that Luke omits the painful details Mark and Matthew give — Christ's three turnings to the sleeping three for sympathy, and his repeated rousing of them; Luke softens the account, recording the sleep but tracing it to sorrow. The expositors observe the warning hidden in the scene: even those who love Christ may, in weakness, fail him in the hour of need, overcome by the very emotions that should have driven them to prayer; the spirit willing, the flesh weak. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to watch against the weakness of the flesh, lest sorrow or weariness master him in the time of trial, and lest he sleep when he should pray. The verse thus records the disciples sleeping for sorrow — overcome while Christ prayed — teaching the weakness of the flesh even in the loving, Luke's tender insight into its cause, and the warning to watch and pray lest the believer fail in the hour of need.
46:
"And said unto them, Why sleep ye? rise and pray, lest ye enter into temptation." The witnesses note Christ's gentle rousing and renewed charge. Ellicott notes "St. Luke is here briefer than the other two records, and omits the threefold prayer and warning, and the words, half-permissive and half of veiled reproof, which bade the disciples at last to sleep on and take their rest." Robertson notes Luke gives the reproach but not "the almost bitter details" of Mark and Matthew. The witnesses note Christ repeats the charge of verse 40 — rise and pray against temptation.

The witnesses note Christ rousing the sleeping disciples and renewing his charge to pray against temptation.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's rousing and renewed charge to pray. The lesson the expositors draw is the persistence of Christ's care for his disciples and the urgency of prayer against the temptation now at hand. The witnesses note Christ's gentle question — "Why sleep ye?" — a reproach, yet tender, rousing the disciples from the sleep that left them unprepared; and his renewed charge — "rise and pray, lest ye enter into temptation" — repeating the very word he had given on entering the garden (v.40). The expositors observe the urgency: the temptation was now upon them — the betrayer at hand, the arrest imminent, the scattering and Peter's denial about to come; and Christ, knowing their peril, rouses them and bids them pray, that they might be kept from being overcome. The witnesses press the persistence of Christ's care: even in his own agony, he thinks of his disciples' danger, and labours for their good — rousing them, charging them again to pray, providing for their keeping though they had failed to watch. The expositors draw the lesson on the renewed charge: prayer is so necessary a safeguard that Christ repeats the command — not once but twice he bids them pray against temptation; the believer cannot be too often urged to the prayer that keeps him in trial. The witnesses note Ellicott's observation that Luke softens the account, omitting the threefold rousing and the half-permissive "sleep on now" of the other Gospels; Luke gives the reproach gently, recording Christ's care without the bitter details of the disciples' repeated failure. The expositors observe the tragedy that the charge went unheeded: the disciples, roused but unprepared by prayer, would within the hour be overcome — fleeing and denying; their failure to pray the cause of their fall. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to heed Christ's charge, rising from spiritual sleep to pray against temptation, especially when the trial is near; for the unprayed-for soul, like the sleeping disciples, falls when the hour comes. The verse thus records Christ's rousing of the disciples and his renewed charge to pray — teaching the persistence of his care, the urgency of prayer against imminent temptation, and the warning that the neglect of prayer leaves the soul exposed to the snare now at hand.
47:
"And while he yet spake, behold a multitude, and he that was called Judas, one of the twelve, went before them, and drew near unto Jesus to kiss him." The witnesses note the arrival of the betrayer at the head of the band. Cyril marks the evangelist's tone: "he says, he that was called Judas, holding his name as it were in abhorrence; but adds, one of the twelve, to signify the enormity of the traitor; for he who had been honoured as an apostle became the cause of the murder of Christ." Chrysostom draws the lesson of the hardened soul: "as incurable wounds yield neither to severe nor soothing remedies, so the soul once taken captive by sin reaps no benefit from admonition; so it was with Judas, who desisted not from his betrayal though deterred by Christ by every warning." The witnesses note Judas leading the multitude and drawing near to kiss Christ.

The witnesses note the betrayer at the head of the band, approaching with the kiss of treachery.

So the witnesses make the verse the arrival of Judas to betray Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the enormity of the betrayer's crime and the hardness of the soul given over to sin. The witnesses note the aggravating detail repeated: "one of the twelve" — Cyril marks that the evangelist names Judas with abhorrence ("he that was called Judas") yet adds "one of the twelve" to signify the horror of the deed: the betrayer was no stranger but an apostle, honoured with the highest privilege, who now became the cause of his Master's murder. The expositors press the dreadfulness: that one of the chosen, who had walked with Christ, witnessed his works, shared his table, should now lead the armed band against him, going before them to point him out. And Judas "drew near unto Jesus to kiss him" — making the token of love and reverence the instrument of betrayal, the deepest hypocrisy crowning the deepest treachery. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's solemn lesson on the hardened heart: Judas had been warned by Christ in every way — by word, by the washing of his feet, by the offered sop, by the revelation at the table — yet none of it moved him; for the soul once captive to sin, like an incurable wound, yields to no remedy, neither severe nor gentle. The expositors draw the warning: the love of money and the consent to sin had so hardened Judas that even Christ's tenderest appeals could not reclaim him; a fearful proof of how sin, indulged, deadens the soul to all warning and grace. The witnesses note the contrast: Christ, who had just prayed in agony and risen to meet his hour, now faces the betrayer with calm; while Judas, hardened and hypocritical, approaches with the false kiss. The verse thus records the arrival of Judas to betray Christ — one of the twelve, with the kiss of treachery — teaching the enormity of his crime, the hardness of the soul given over to sin, and the dreadful possibility that the highest privilege, abused, may end in the basest betrayal.
48:
"But Jesus said unto him, Judas, betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?" The witnesses note Christ's searching word to the betrayer. The Reformed Gill marks the naming: "calling him by his name, that he might know he knew him, and to aggravate his sin — what, Judas, my apostle, my friend, my familiar friend, in whom I trusted." Barnes presses the emphasis of every word: "Betrayest thou — dost thou violate all thy obligations of fidelity? Betrayest thou — thou, so long with him, so much favoured? Betrayest thou the Son of man — the Messiah, the world's Redeemer? Betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss — the sign of friendship and affection?" The witnesses note Christ exposing the treachery and hypocrisy of the kiss.

The witnesses note Christ's tender yet searching reproach: betraying the Son of man with the kiss of love.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's searching word to Judas. The lesson the expositors draw is the tender severity of Christ's reproach, exposing the depth of the treachery while still appealing to the traitor's conscience. The witnesses note that Christ names him — "Judas" — Gill marking the force: by naming him, Christ shows that he knows him fully, knows the deed and the heart; and the name recalls all that Judas was — apostle, friend, familiar companion, one trusted by Christ — heightening the horror of the betrayal. The expositors press Barnes's analysis of the emphatic words: "betrayest thou the Son of man with a kiss?" — every word a reproach. "Betrayest thou" — dost thou, of all men, violate every bond of fidelity and deliver up thy Master? "Thou" — so long with him, so favoured, so sure he is the Messiah? "The Son of man" — the Christ, the Redeemer of the world, the hope of nations? "With a kiss" — turning the very sign of love and friendship into the badge of betrayal? The witnesses observe the searching force: Christ lays bare the full enormity of the deed, the treachery, the ingratitude, the hypocrisy, in one piercing question. Yet, the expositors note, the word is not mere condemnation but a final appeal: Christ, even now, addresses Judas tenderly by name, exposing his sin that he might, even at this last moment, feel its horror and repent — the patient love that sought the betrayer's soul to the very end. The witnesses press the dreadfulness of the kiss: that Judas should make the kiss — the token of affection and reverence — the instrument of his treachery is the deepest hypocrisy; and Christ's question fastens on it, "with a kiss?" — exposing the falseness that profaned love itself. The expositors draw the lesson on the betrayal of Christ under the forms of love: how often men profess affection for Christ while betraying him in the heart, kissing him with the lips while delivering him up by their deeds; the kiss of Judas the type of all false profession. The verse thus records Christ's searching word to Judas — betraying the Son of man with a kiss — teaching the tender severity of his reproach, the exposure of the treachery and hypocrisy, and the final appeal of love to the conscience of the betrayer.
49:
"When they which were about him saw what would follow, they said unto him, Lord, shall we smite with the sword?" The witnesses note the disciples' question and Luke's distinctive detail. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes "the phrase is apparently chosen as more accurate than the disciples would have been; those who spoke were probably the three nearest." Robertson notes "Luke alone gives this question," and that the disciples "had the two swords already mentioned" (v.38); they ask leave to use them. The witnesses note the disciples, seeing the arrest at hand, ask whether to resist with the sword.

The witnesses note the disciples' question: seeing the arrest coming, should they smite with the sword?

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' question about resistance. The lesson the expositors draw is the disciples' carnal impulse to defend Christ by force, and the contrast with his way of submission. The witnesses note that "they which were about him saw what would follow" — perceiving that the band had come to seize Christ, the disciples (probably the three nearest, as Ellicott suggests) ask, "Lord, shall we smite with the sword?" — proposing to resist the arrest by force, with the two swords they had. The expositors observe the carnal misunderstanding this betrays: the disciples, who had taken Christ's word about the sword literally (v.38), now think the hour has come to use it; they would defend their Master by violence, not understanding that he went willingly to his Passion and would not be saved by the sword. The witnesses press the contrast with Christ's way: the disciples reach for the weapon, but Christ's kingdom is not defended by carnal arms (John 18:36); his way is submission to the Father's will, not resistance to the betrayer. The expositors draw the lesson: the impulse to defend Christ and his cause by force is a carnal one, springing from a misunderstanding of his kingdom; the servant of Christ is not to take the sword, for those who take it perish by it (Matthew 26:52). The witnesses note the detail peculiar to Luke: Luke alone records the disciples' question, "shall we smite?" — asked before Peter acted; and Robertson observes that Peter, impetuous, did not wait for the answer but struck (the next verse), acting before Christ could reply. The expositors observe the well-meant but mistaken zeal: the disciples loved Christ and would protect him, but their zeal was carnal and contrary to his purpose; their love real, their method wrong. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is not to defend Christ's cause by violence or worldly weapons, but by the spiritual means Christ appoints; the sword is not the way of his kingdom. The verse thus records the disciples' question about resistance — shall we smite with the sword? — teaching their carnal impulse to defend Christ by force, the contrast with his way of submission, and the lesson that the cause of Christ is not to be advanced by the sword.
50:
"And one of them smote the servant of the high priest, and cut off his right ear." The witnesses note Peter's rash blow and Luke the physician's precision. The Reformed Gill identifies the actors: "the person that drew his sword and performed this daring action, not waiting for an answer from Christ, was Peter, and the high priest's servant that he smote was Malchus" — both names learned from John (18:10). The grammatical-historical Robertson marks the medical detail: "his right ear — Mark and Matthew do not mention 'right,' but Luke the Physician does; John follows Luke in this item." The witnesses note Peter striking the servant and cutting off his ear.

The witnesses note Peter's rash blow: cutting off the right ear of the high priest's servant.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's rash use of the sword. The lesson the expositors draw is the impetuous, misguided zeal of Peter, and the precision of Luke's eyewitness account. The witnesses note that "one of them" — Peter, as John reveals (18:10) — did not wait for Christ's answer to the disciples' question, but, impetuous as ever, drew his sword and struck, cutting off the right ear of Malchus, the high priest's servant. The expositors press the rashness: Peter, who had boasted he would die for Christ, now acts on that boast in the wrong way — striking with the sword before Christ could reply, his zeal outrunning his wisdom; the same self-confidence that would soon fail in denial now flares in violence. The witnesses observe the detail peculiar to Luke, which Robertson marks: Luke alone (followed by John) notes it was the "right" ear — the physician-evangelist's precision, his eye for the medical particular; a small token of the eyewitness accuracy of the account. The expositors draw the lesson on misguided zeal: Peter's love for Christ was real, but his method was carnal and contrary to Christ's will; he would defend his Master by the sword, when Christ went willingly to his Passion and would heal the very wound Peter inflicted. The witnesses note that the disciples' names (Peter, Malchus) are given by John but not the Synoptists — John writing later, after Peter's death, when it was safe to name him; Luke records the deed and the wounded man's ear, but not the names. The expositors observe the providence: Peter's rash blow gave occasion for the last miracle of Christ before his Passion — the healing of the ear (the next verse) — turning even his disciple's error to a display of mercy. The witnesses draw the application: the believer's zeal for Christ must be governed by wisdom and submission to his will; rash, carnal action, however well-meant, is not the way of Christ, and may do harm that mercy must repair. The verse thus records Peter's rash blow — cutting off Malchus's right ear — teaching the impetuous, misguided zeal of the disciple, the precision of Luke's eyewitness account, and the prelude to the healing mercy of Christ.
51:
"And Jesus answered and said, Suffer ye thus far. And he touched his ear, and healed him." The witnesses note Christ's word and the healing of the ear, peculiar to Luke. Clarke offers the rendering and the act: "Suffer me to go thus far — as they had a firm hold of Christ, he wished them to permit him to go as far as Malchus, that he might heal his ear"; or, addressed to the disciples, "Let them proceed; make no resistance." Ellicott notes "the words and the incident are peculiar to St. Luke," and that the word may be a command to the disciples to be patient or a plea to the captors. The witnesses note Christ healing the very wound his disciple had inflicted — the last miracle before his Passion.

The witnesses note Christ's mercy: healing the ear Peter cut off, even on his enemies, as he went to his arrest.

So the witnesses make the verse the healing of Malchus's ear. The lesson the expositors draw is the wonderful mercy of Christ, who healed the wound inflicted by his disciple even upon one of his captors, displaying his compassion to the last. The witnesses note the word "Suffer ye thus far" — variously read: as a command to the disciples to cease their resistance and let things take their course; or as a plea to the captors to allow him a moment; or (Clarke) as "suffer me to go thus far," that he might reach and heal the wounded man. The expositors press the act that follows: "he touched his ear, and healed him" — Christ, in the very moment of his arrest, with the band laying hold of him, stooped to heal the ear that Peter had cut off; an act of pure mercy, shown not to a friend but to one of his enemies, a servant come to seize him. The witnesses observe the wonder Ambrose had noted (on the arrest): such was Christ's compassion that "even His enemies He suffered not to be wounded" — he undid the harm his disciple had done, sparing his captor. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's mercy: even as he went to his Passion, betrayed and seized, he did good to his enemies, healing the wounded; his compassion failed not even in his own extremity, but reached to those who came against him. The witnesses note that this was the last miracle of Christ before his death — a miracle of healing, and of healing an enemy — a fitting close to a ministry of mercy, and a token that he went to the cross still doing good. The expositors observe the rebuke to Peter implicit in the healing: Christ undoes the disciple's rash deed, showing by his act that the sword was not his way, and that mercy, not violence, marks his kingdom. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to follow his Lord in returning good for evil, in healing rather than wounding, in mercy even to enemies; and to marvel at a Saviour whose compassion reached, in his own agony, to the one who came to arrest him. The verse thus records the healing of Malchus's ear — Christ's mercy to his enemy — teaching the wonderful compassion of Christ to the last, the rebuke of carnal violence, and the love that did good even to those who seized him for the cross.
52:
"Then Jesus said unto the chief priests, and captains of the temple, and the elders, which were come to him, Be ye come out, as against a thief, with swords and staves?" The witnesses note Christ's rebuke of the manner of his arrest and a detail of Luke. Ellicott notes "St. Luke stands alone in recording the presence of the men of higher rank" — the chief priests, captains, and elders themselves — "with the officers and multitude." The grammatical-historical Robertson renders "as against a robber" — "they were treating Jesus as if he were a bandit like Barabbas." The witnesses note Christ exposing the absurdity and injustice of the armed, stealthy arrest.

The witnesses note Christ's rebuke: coming against him with swords and staves, as against a robber.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's rebuke of the manner of his arrest. The lesson the expositors draw is the absurdity and injustice of the armed, stealthy seizure of the innocent Christ. The witnesses note that Christ addressed not the rabble only but "the chief priests, and captains of the temple, and the elders" — and Ellicott marks that Luke alone records the presence of these men of rank, who had come in person to the arrest, lending their authority to the deed. Christ's question, the expositors press, exposes the injustice: "Be ye come out, as against a thief, with swords and staves?" — they came armed, with weapons and clubs, by night, as though to take a dangerous robber or bandit (Robertson: treating him like Barabbas); yet he was no robber, no man of violence, but the Teacher who had offered no resistance and threatened no one. The witnesses observe the absurdity the question lays bare: the armed band, the swords and staves, the stealthy midnight seizure, were all unnecessary against one who went willingly and resisted not; the show of force betrayed both the injustice of the charge and the cowardice of the proceeding. The expositors draw the lesson: the manner of the arrest condemned the arresters — coming by night, armed, against an unarmed and innocent man, as against a criminal; their very weapons a witness against them. The witnesses note the dignity of Christ in the rebuke: not pleading for himself, but exposing the wickedness of his enemies, calmly questioning the injustice of their armed assault. The expositors observe the contrast: Christ, who had taught openly in the temple (the next verse), is taken by stealth and force, as though a felon; the open Teacher seized like a hidden criminal. The witnesses draw the application: the truth has often been treated as a crime, and its messengers seized as malefactors; but the injustice of such treatment condemns the persecutors, not the persecuted. The verse thus records Christ's rebuke of the manner of his arrest — armed, as against a robber — teaching the absurdity and injustice of the seizure, the guilt of the rulers who came in person, and the calm dignity of Christ exposing the wickedness of his enemies.
53:
"When I was daily with you in the temple, ye stretched forth no hands against me: but this is your hour, and the power of darkness." The witnesses note the exposure of the rulers' cowardice and the meaning of "the power of darkness." The Reformed Gill notes Christ "had been for some days past teaching the people" openly in the temple, yet they took him not. Lightfoot expounds "this is your hour, and the power of darkness": "the serpent himself is now come in Judas... now was the hour for that wickedness; it was anciently foretold and predetermined; and now all fences lie open... the chains of the devil himself are now loosed, and it is permitted to him, without restraint of Divine Providence, to exert all his furies." The witnesses note Christ's word that this was the appointed hour of darkness, permitted for a season.

The witnesses note Christ exposing the cowardice of his enemies and naming the appointed "hour and power of darkness."

So the witnesses make the verse the close of Christ's rebuke and the naming of the hour of darkness. The lesson the expositors draw is the cowardice of the enemies, exposed by their stealth, and the appointed permission of evil in "the power of darkness." The witnesses note the first part — "When I was daily with you in the temple, ye stretched forth no hands against me" — Christ exposing the cowardice of his enemies: he had taught openly, day after day, in the temple, where they might have taken him had their cause been just; yet they dared not, fearing the people, and now came by stealth and by night. The expositors press the indictment: their resort to a midnight, armed seizure, when they had passed up every chance to take him openly, convicts them of injustice and cowardice — their charge would not bear the light, and so they worked in darkness. But the deeper word, the witnesses observe, is "this is your hour, and the power of darkness."
