Christ names the appointed time: this is "your hour" — the brief season permitted to the enemies to work their will; and "the power of darkness" — the time when the powers of evil, the devil and his instruments, are allowed to exert themselves. Lightfoot draws out the meaning: the serpent had come in Judas, the chains of the devil were for a moment loosed, and it was permitted to him to pour out his fury without the usual restraint of providence — the hour foreordained for the triumph (so it seemed) of darkness. The expositors draw the lesson: the Passion was the hour of darkness, when God permitted the powers of evil their seeming victory — yet only their "hour," a brief and bounded season, after which the light would conquer. The witnesses note the comfort hidden in the limitation: it was "your hour" — a passing hour, not a lasting dominion; the power of darkness was permitted, but bounded, and would be overthrown by the resurrection. The expositors observe the sovereignty even here: the enemies and the devil could do nothing but what was permitted, in the appointed hour; God overruled even the power of darkness to accomplish redemption. The witnesses draw the application: there are hours when evil seems to prevail and darkness to reign, permitted for a season; but they are bounded, and God's purpose is served even through them. The verse thus records Christ's naming of the hour of darkness — the cowardice of the enemies exposed, the appointed permission of evil — teaching the injustice of his foes, the bounded season of darkness, and the sovereignty of God overruling even the power of darkness for the redemption of the world.
54:
"Then took they him, and led him, and brought him into the high priest's house. And Peter followed afar off." The witnesses note the seizure of Christ and Peter's distant following. Ambrose guards the truth of the incarnation: "when we read of Jesus being holden, let us guard against thinking that He is holden with respect to His divine nature, and unwilling through weakness, for He is held captive and bound according to the truth of His bodily nature." Augustine harmonizes the accounts: "first He was led to Annas, then to Caiaphas." Chrysostom commends Peter's courage in following at all, when the others fled, yet the "afar off" marks his weakness. The witnesses note Peter following at a distance — love drawing him on, fear holding him back.

The witnesses note Christ led away captive, and Peter following afar off — the seed of his denial.

So the witnesses make the verse the seizure of Christ and Peter's distant following. The lesson the expositors draw is the willing captivity of Christ in his human nature, and the dangerous middle course of Peter, following afar off. The witnesses note the seizure: "then took they him, and led him... into the high priest's house" — Christ, who had healed his captor's ear, now suffers himself to be bound and led away. Ambrose presses the careful truth: Christ is "holden" not in his divine nature (which is free and almighty) nor unwillingly through weakness, but according to the truth of his bodily nature — he submits to be taken as a real man, freely yielding to the arrest, his deity never compromised. The expositors observe the willing captivity: he who could have summoned legions of angels allows himself to be bound, going as a lamb to the slaughter; his being taken is the proof of his voluntary submission, not of any weakness. The witnesses turn to Peter: "Peter followed afar off." The expositors note the mixture in this — Chrysostom commends his courage, for he followed when the others fled, his love drawing him on; yet the "afar off" betrays his fear, holding him back from openly standing with Christ. The witnesses press the danger of this middle course: Peter followed, but at a distance — neither boldly confessing his Lord nor safely apart, but in the perilous middle, near enough to be questioned, too fearful to confess; and this following afar off, the expositors note, was the seed of his denial — he who would not stand near Christ would soon deny him. The witnesses draw the lesson: the disciple who follows Christ "afar off," half-ashamed, half-afraid, neither openly confessing nor wholly forsaking, is in the greatest danger of falling; the safe place is near Christ, in open confession, not at the distance of fear. The expositors observe the providence: Peter's distant following brought him to the high priest's court, where his fall would come. The verse thus records the seizure of Christ and Peter's distant following — teaching the willing captivity of Christ in his manhood, and the perilous middle course of Peter, whose following afar off was the prelude to his denial.
55:
"And when they had kindled a fire in the midst of the hall, and were set down together, Peter sat down among them." The witnesses note the fire and Peter's seating among the enemies. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects "hall" to "the court-yard" (an open space), and Robertson explains "kindled a fire" (a blaze) — "it was April and cool at night; the servants made the fire" — and that all "had taken seats around the fire," Peter among them. The witnesses note Peter, having followed afar, now sits down among the servants and officers at their fire.

The witnesses note Peter seated among the enemies at the fire — placing himself in the way of temptation.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's seating among the enemies. The lesson the expositors draw is the imprudence of Peter, who placed himself among the foes of Christ, in the very way of temptation. The witnesses note the scene: a fire kindled in the open court-yard (Ellicott corrects "hall" to "court-yard"), for it was a cool April night; the servants and officers gathered round it; and "Peter sat down among them." The expositors press the danger of this seating: Peter, who had followed afar off, now goes further into peril — sitting down among the very enemies of Christ, in the company of those who had seized his Master, warming himself at their fire. The witnesses observe the imprudence: having neither boldly confessed Christ nor withdrawn to safety, Peter placed himself in the midst of temptation — among hostile servants, in the firelight that would expose his face, where he would be questioned and pressed to deny. The expositors draw the lesson, echoing the warning of the foregoing chapter (Luke 22:40): Peter, who had failed to watch and pray, now walks into the snare; he seeks warmth and concealment among the enemies, and finds instead the occasion of his fall. The witnesses note the spiritual peril of such company: the disciple who sits down among the foes of Christ, seeking the world's warmth and fellowship, exposes himself to the temptation to deny his Lord; bad company and the desire to be inconspicuous among enemies are the road to denial. The expositors observe the contrast Peter should have kept: not following afar and sitting among foes, but standing openly with Christ, or withdrawing to pray; instead he chose the middle, perilous course. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to beware of placing himself in the way of temptation, among the enemies of Christ, seeking the world's company and warmth; for such imprudence, joined to prayerlessness, is the prelude to denial. The verse thus records Peter's seating among the enemies at the fire — teaching the imprudence that places the soul in the way of temptation, the danger of seeking the world's fellowship among Christ's foes, and the further step of Peter toward the denial now at hand.
56:
"But a certain maid beheld him as he sat by the fire, and earnestly looked upon him, and said, This man was also with him." The witnesses note the first challenge and the betraying firelight. The grammatical-historical Clarke and Ellicott note the maid "earnestly looked upon him" — Luke's characteristic word for a fixed gaze (cf. Luke 4:20) — "by the help of the light of the fire at which Peter sat." Ellicott notes the "also" is explained by John's narrative (the other disciple already known). The witnesses note the maid, by the firelight, recognizing Peter as one of Christ's followers.

The witnesses note the first assault: a servant-girl, by the firelight, marking Peter as a follower of Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the first challenge to Peter. The lesson the expositors draw is how the firelight Peter sought for warmth betrayed him, and how the least of accusers began his fall. The witnesses note that "a certain maid beheld him as he sat by the fire, and earnestly looked upon him" — fixing her gaze on him (Luke's characteristic word for a steady look), and, by the help of the firelight, recognizing him as one of Christ's company; "This man was also with him." The expositors press the irony: the fire Peter sought for warmth and the company he joined for concealment became the very means of his exposure — the firelight fell on his face, and the maid, looking earnestly, marked him out; what he meant for safety betrayed him. The witnesses observe the smallness of the accuser: it was not a magistrate or armed officer, but a servant-girl, the least and weakest of challengers, whose word began Peter's fall — the bold disciple, who had drawn the sword against the band, now to be overthrown by the look and word of a maid. The expositors draw the lesson on the suddenness and smallness of temptation: the trial that overcame Peter came not in the form he had braced for (prison and death, as he boasted) but in a slight, unexpected form — a girl's casual recognition; and the great may fall before the small when they are unprepared. The witnesses note the "also" that Ellicott explains: the maid says Peter was "also" with Christ, the word implying another already known (John, the other disciple, who had brought Peter in); so the recognition spread from one to another. The expositors observe the providence and the warning: Peter, imprudent and prayerless, is now in the snare; the firelight exposes him, the maid challenges him, and the denial is about to come. The witnesses draw the application: the believer must beware that the very means he seeks for safety or concealment may betray him, and that temptation often comes in small, unexpected forms; vigilance is needed against the least as well as the greatest assault. The verse thus records the first challenge to Peter — the maid by the firelight — teaching how the fire he sought betrayed him, how the least accuser began his fall, and the suddenness of the temptation that would overthrow the unprepared disciple.
57:
"And he denied him, saying, Woman, I know him not." The witnesses note Peter's first denial. The Reformed Gill marks the suddenness of the fall: "startled at the maid's positive assertion, and knowing not how to clear himself any other way, roundly, and at once, denied that Jesus was his master... and thus he that had said he would die with him, rather than deny him, is frighted at a servant maid, and denies him upon the first attack." Robertson notes "I know him not" — "just as Jesus had predicted." The witnesses note Peter, at the first challenge, denying that he knew Christ.

The witnesses note Peter's first denial: at a maid's word, disowning all knowledge of his Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's first denial. The lesson the expositors draw is the swiftness and shamefulness of Peter's fall, and the contrast between his boast and his failure. The witnesses note the directness of the denial: "Woman, I know him not" — Peter, at the maid's accusation, disowns all knowledge of Christ, denying that he was his disciple or even knew him; the plainest, most outright denial. The expositors press the contrast Gill draws: this is the same Peter who, but hours before, had boasted that he would go with Christ to prison and to death, who had drawn the sword against the armed band; and now, at the first attack, frighted not by soldiers but by a servant-girl, he "roundly, and at once" denies his Lord. The witnesses observe the shamefulness of the fall: not after long pressure, not before a tribunal, but at the first challenge, from the weakest of accusers, Peter denied — the boldness of his boast collapsing at the first breath of danger. The expositors draw the lesson on self-confidence: Peter's fall is the fruit of the self-confidence he had shown; he who trusted his own strength, who could not conceive of denying Christ, fails utterly at the first test — for "let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall" (1 Corinthians 10:12). The witnesses note the fulfilment of Christ's word, which Robertson marks: "I know him not" — the very denial Christ had foretold (Luke 22:34); the exact prophecy now exactly fulfilled, confirming Christ's foreknowledge even in the disciple's sin. The expositors observe the depth of the denial: not a mere evasion, but the disowning of all knowledge of Christ — the disciple denying his Lord, the rock crumbling at the first wave. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is warned by Peter's fall against self-confidence and prayerlessness; the strongest, trusting himself and unprepared by prayer, may fall at the slightest temptation, denying the Lord he thought he could never forsake. The verse thus records Peter's first denial — "I know him not" — teaching the swiftness and shame of his fall, the collapse of his self-confident boast, and the exact fulfilment of Christ's foreknowing word.
58:
"And after a little while another saw him, and said, Thou art also of them. And Peter said, Man, I am not." The witnesses note the second challenge and denial. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the vocative "Man" "always implies a certain touch of anger or impatience" (cf. Luke 12:14). Robertson and Wesley address the harmonizing of the four accounts: "it is almost impossible to co-ordinate the three denials unless we conceive of several joining in when one led off"; here "another" is a man, where Matthew and Mark mention a maid — divers persons concurring in charging Peter. The witnesses note Peter's second, blunter denial — "I am not."

The witnesses note the second assault and Peter's curt denial, his fall deepening.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's second denial. The lesson the expositors draw is the deepening of Peter's fall under renewed pressure, and the harmony of the Gospel accounts in the multiplied accusers. The witnesses note the second challenge: "after a little while another saw him, and said, Thou art also of them" — the accusation renewed by another, the pressure mounting; and Peter answers, "Man, I am not" — a curt, impatient denial, Ellicott noting that the address "Man" carries a touch of anger or vexation. The expositors observe the deepening of the fall: the first denial led to the second; having once denied, Peter is drawn further, and now answers more bluntly, with rising irritation — sin breeding sin, the first lie requiring more. The witnesses press the harmonizing question that Robertson and Wesley address: the four Gospels give varying details of the accusers (here "another" man, in Matthew and Mark a maid, in John various), and the reconciliation, the expositors note, is that several persons joined in charging Peter, the accusation taken up by one and another, maid and man alike, as the suspicion spread; so the accounts do not contradict but supplement one another, each recording different voices in the same mounting assault. The witnesses observe the realism: such a scene, with a suspected man pressed by a crowd, would naturally draw several accusers, and the Gospels faithfully record the varied challenges. The expositors draw the lesson on the progress of sin: Peter, having denied once, cannot now retreat; the second challenge finds him further committed, and he denies again, more sharply — the soul, once it begins to deny, is carried on, each step harder to retrace. The witnesses note the contrast with the proper course: had Peter, at the first denial, repented and confessed, he might have stopped his fall; but, drawn on, he denies again, sinking deeper. The expositors observe the mercy yet to come: even this deepening fall would not be final, for Christ had prayed for Peter (Luke 22:32). The witnesses draw the application: the believer who falls into one sin is in danger of being carried into more; the way of safety is to turn back at once, not to be drawn deeper by the pressure of temptation. The verse thus records Peter's second denial — "I am not" — teaching the deepening of his fall under renewed assault, the harmony of the Gospels in the multiplied accusers, and the progress of sin that carries the soul from one denial to another.
59:
"And about the space of one hour after another confidently affirmed, saying, Of a truth this fellow also was with him: for he is a Galilaean." The witnesses note the third challenge and its ground. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "confidently affirmed" (he kept affirming strongly), and "for he is a Galilean" — Peter's accent betraying him (cf. Matthew 26:73). Ellicott notes the words for the hour's interval and the strong affirmation are "peculiar to St. Luke." The witnesses note the third accuser, an hour later, insisting Peter was Christ's follower, his Galilean speech the proof.

The witnesses note the third assault: an hour later, a confident charge grounded on Peter's Galilean accent.

So the witnesses make the verse the third challenge to Peter. The lesson the expositors draw is the mounting pressure toward the climax of Peter's fall, and the irony that his own speech betrayed him. The witnesses note the interval — "about the space of one hour after" — Luke's precise note (Robertson marks the word peculiar to him); and the third accuser, who "confidently affirmed," kept insisting strongly, "Of a truth this fellow also was with him: for he is a Galilaean." The expositors observe the ground of this charge: Peter's Galilean accent, his manner of speech, gave him away (as Matthew notes, "thy speech bewrayeth thee," 26:73); the dialect of Galilee was distinct, and marked him as a countryman of Jesus. The witnesses press the irony: Peter's own tongue, by which he denied Christ, betrayed him as Christ's disciple; the very speech he used to disown his Lord proclaimed his connection with him. The expositors draw out the mounting pressure: the third challenge is the most confident and public — "confidently affirmed," before the company, with the evidence of his speech; the suspicion now hardened into assured accusation, the net closing around Peter. The witnesses note the climax approaching: after the first denial (to the maid) and the second (to the man), now comes the third and strongest assault, an hour later, with the damning evidence of his accent; Peter is cornered, and his third and deepest denial (with cursing and swearing, the next verse) is at hand. The expositors observe the realism of the account: the hour's interval, the renewed and stronger charge, the evidence of speech — all true to the natural course of such a scene, and faithfully recorded. The witnesses draw the lesson: sin, persisted in, brings mounting pressure and deeper entanglement; Peter, having denied twice, is now pressed hardest, and his fall reaches its depth. The expositors note the providence that the cock would crow at this third denial, recalling Christ's word. The witnesses draw the application: the believer who begins to deny is carried to the climax of his fall under mounting pressure; only repentance can break the descent. The verse thus records the third challenge — the confident charge grounded on Peter's accent — teaching the mounting pressure toward the climax of his fall, and the irony that his own speech betrayed the Lord he was denying with it.
60:
"And Peter said, Man, I know not what thou sayest. And immediately, while he yet spake, the cock crew." The witnesses note the third denial and the crowing cock. The Reformed Gill notes Peter "then began to curse and swear, and wish the most dreadful things to befall him, if he knew any thing of Jesus" (as Matthew and Mark record). The grammatical-historical Robertson observes "each denial tangles Peter more and more," and "Peter could hear the crowing all right" — the cock crowing the very moment he spoke. The witnesses note Peter's third and deepest denial, and the immediate cock-crow.

The witnesses note Peter's third denial, sealed with the crowing of the cock at the very moment.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of Peter's denial. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of Peter's third denial and the appointed sign of the cock-crow that would break his heart. The witnesses note the denial: "Man, I know not what thou sayest" — Peter feigning that he does not even understand the charge, the third and most vehement disowning; and (as the other Gospels record, Gill notes) he reinforced it with cursing and swearing, calling down dreadful things on himself if he knew Christ — the deepest depth of the fall, the apostle profanely abjuring his Lord. The expositors press the progress Robertson marks: "each denial tangles Peter more and more" — from the simple denial to the maid, to the blunter denial to the man, to this vehement, profane denial with oaths; sin carrying him down step by step into ever deeper guilt. The witnesses observe the climax of the fall: Peter, who had walked with Christ, seen his glory on the mount, confessed him the Son of God, now denies with curses that he even knows him — the rock crumbled, the boldest fallen lowest. But at this very moment, the expositors note, "while he yet spake, the cock crew" — the appointed sign, sounding the instant his denial was complete; the cock-crow that Christ had foretold (Luke 22:34), now fulfilled to the moment, breaking in upon Peter's profane oaths. The witnesses press the providence and the mercy of the sign: the crowing cock, ordained beforehand, was the means by which Peter would be recalled — the sound that, with the Lord's look (the next verse), would pierce his heart and bring him to repentance. The expositors draw the lesson: even in the depth of the fall, God provides the means of recovery; the cock-crow, the look of Christ, the prayer of Christ (v.32) — all converge to restore the fallen disciple. The witnesses note the exact fulfilment of Christ's word, confirming his foreknowledge even in Peter's sin. The verse thus records the climax of Peter's denial and the crowing cock — the deepest fall met by the appointed sign — teaching the depth to which sin carries the soul, the exact fulfilment of Christ's word, and the mercy that, even in the fall, provides the means of recovery.
61:
"And the Lord turned, and looked upon Peter. And Peter remembered the word of the Lord, how he had said unto him, Before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice." The witnesses note the Lord's look — the turning-point of Peter's life, peculiar to Luke. Ellicott marks it: "the glance which was thus the turning point of Peter's life is mentioned only by St. Luke; as he was being led from Annas to the more public trial, our Lord must have looked on the disciple." Clarke calls it "this delicate reproof." The witnesses note that the Lord's look recalled to Peter the forgotten word, and broke his heart.

The witnesses note the Lord's look that pierced Peter, recalling the word and awakening repentance.

So the witnesses make the verse the Lord's look that recalled Peter. The lesson the expositors draw is the power of Christ's gracious look to recall the fallen disciple to repentance. The witnesses note the moment: "the Lord turned, and looked upon Peter" — at the very instant of the cock-crow, Christ, being led from one trial to another, turned and fixed his eyes on Peter; a look, Ellicott marks, recorded only by Luke, yet the turning-point of Peter's life. The expositors press the power of that look: it was a look of reproof, yet of love — not of anger or condemnation, but of grieved tenderness; the Lord, even in his own suffering, turned to seek his fallen disciple, and that one glance pierced Peter's heart where the cock-crow alone might not have. The witnesses observe the effect: "Peter remembered the word of the Lord" — the look recalled the forgotten prophecy ("before the cock crow, thou shalt deny me thrice"); the cock-crow and the look together brought the warning flooding back, and Peter saw his sin in the light of Christ's foreknowing word. The expositors draw the lesson on the means of repentance: it was the look of Christ that broke Peter — the gracious, sorrowful gaze of the Lord he had denied, recalling the word he had forgotten; so the sinner is brought to repentance by the gracious dealing of Christ, who looks upon the fallen in love, recalls his word, and awakens the conscience. The witnesses note the tenderness Clarke marks: "this delicate reproof" — Christ does not rebuke Peter with words before the crowd, but with a look; a silent, piercing, loving reproof that did what no word could. The expositors observe the contrast with Judas: Peter, looked upon by Christ and recalling his word, repents and weeps; Judas, hardened, despairs and perishes — the difference being the grace of Christ that recalled Peter. The witnesses draw the application: the fallen believer is recalled by the gracious look of Christ and the remembrance of his word; let the sinner heed that look, remember the word, and turn in repentance. The verse thus records the Lord's look that recalled Peter — the turning-point of his life — teaching the power of Christ's gracious, sorrowful look to break the heart of the fallen, recall his word, and lead to repentance.
62:
"And Peter went out, and wept bitterly." The witnesses note Peter's penitent tears. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes the ingressive aorist — "he burst into tears" — and that "bitter is a common expression for tears in all languages and in all hearts." Barnes cross-references the penitential cries of the Psalms and the godly sorrow that works repentance (Psalm 130:1-4; 2 Corinthians 7:10-11). The witnesses note Peter, broken by the look and the word, going out to weep in bitter repentance.

The witnesses note Peter's bitter weeping — the godly sorrow of a truly penitent heart.

So the witnesses make the verse Peter's penitent tears. The lesson the expositors draw is the godly sorrow of Peter, whose bitter weeping proved the truth of his repentance and the difference between the fallen restored and the lost. The witnesses note that "Peter went out" — leaving the court of his denial, withdrawing from the company of the enemies — "and wept bitterly"; Robertson marks the force, "he burst into tears," the dam of his grief breaking. The expositors press the depth of the sorrow: Peter's tears were not the tears of mere remorse or fear, but of godly sorrow — the broken-hearted grief of one who has wounded the Lord he loves; he wept bitterly, the bitterness measuring the depth of his penitence and his love. The witnesses observe what brought the tears: the Lord's look and the remembered word (v.61) pierced his heart, and the sight of his sin against so loving a Master overwhelmed him; he went out and wept, unable to bear the company where he had denied his Lord. The expositors draw the lesson on godly sorrow: Peter's weeping is the pattern of true repentance — the sorrow that works repentance unto salvation, not to be repented of (2 Corinthians 7:10); the broken and contrite heart that God will not despise (Psalm 51:17). The witnesses note the contrast Barnes implies with Judas: both betrayed Christ, both felt remorse; but Peter wept bitterly and was restored, while Judas despaired and perished — the difference being that Peter's was godly sorrow, working repentance and turning him back to Christ, while Judas's was the sorrow of the world, working death. The expositors observe the fruit of Christ's intercession (Luke 22:32): it was Christ's prayer that kept Peter's faith from failing, so that his fall issued not in despair but in repentance and recovery; the bitter tears the evidence that the prayer was answered. The witnesses draw the application: the fallen believer is to follow Peter — to weep bitterly over his sin, to sorrow with the godly sorrow that turns to Christ, and to find, through repentance, restoration. The verse thus records Peter's bitter weeping — the godly sorrow of a penitent heart — teaching the truth of his repentance, the difference between the restored and the lost, and the fruit of Christ's intercession in the recovery of the fallen disciple.
63:
"And the men that held Jesus mocked him, and smote him." The witnesses note the mocking of Christ and its order in Luke. Augustine harmonizes: "the temptation of Peter is not related by all in the same order; Matthew and Mark first mention the mockings, then Peter's denial, but Luke first describes Peter's denial, then the mockings." Chrysostom draws the lesson: "Jesus, the Lord of heaven and earth, sustains and suffers the mockings of the ungodly, giving us an example of patience." Theophylact notes "the Lord of prophets is derided as a false prophet." The witnesses note Christ, held by his captors, mocked and struck.

The witnesses note Christ enduring the mockery and blows of his captors with patience.

So the witnesses make the verse the mocking of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the patient endurance of Christ under the mockery and abuse of the ungodly, and the example it sets. The witnesses note that "the men that held Jesus mocked him, and smote him" — the captors, having him in their power, heaped on him insult and blows, deriding the One they had bound. The expositors press the wonder Chrysostom marks: "Jesus, the Lord of heaven and earth, sustains and suffers the mockings of the ungodly" — the Creator mocked by his creatures, the Lord of glory struck by the hands he had made; and he bears it all in silence, "giving us an example of patience." The witnesses observe the irony Theophylact notes: "the Lord of prophets is derided as a false prophet" — the very source of all prophecy mocked as a pretender, blindfolded and challenged to prophesy (the next verse); the deepest mockery, that the Truth should be jeered as a deceiver. The expositors draw the lesson on Christ's patience: he who could have summoned legions of angels, who could have struck his mockers dead with a word, endures their insults and blows without retaliation — the supreme example of the patience he had taught, returning not evil for evil, but suffering meekly for our sakes. The witnesses note Augustine's harmonizing of the order: Luke places Peter's denial before the mocking, where Matthew and Mark reverse it — not a contradiction, but the different arrangement of the evangelists, who recorded the same events in varying order; the truth is the same, the sequence differing. The expositors observe the depth of the humiliation: the Son of God, bound and helpless (in appearance), mocked and beaten by his captors through the night — the beginning of the sufferings that would issue in the cross. The witnesses draw the application: the believer, mocked and abused for Christ, is to follow his Master's example of patient endurance, bearing reproach meekly as he did; and to marvel at the love that endured such mockery for our redemption. The verse thus records the mocking of Christ — endured with patience — teaching the meek endurance of the Lord under the abuse of the ungodly, the example of patience he set, and the depth of the humiliation he bore for us.
64:
"And when they had blindfolded him, they struck him on the face, and asked him, saying, Prophesy, who is it that smote thee?" The witnesses note the blindfolding and the mock of his prophetic office. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "blindfolded" (to put a veil around), and Ellicott observes the taunt rests on "the popular view... that identified prophecy with clairvoyance." Wesley notes this abuse was repeated "both before and after his condemnation." The witnesses note the captors veiling Christ's eyes, striking him, and mockingly demanding he prophesy who struck him.

The witnesses note the cruel game: blindfolding Christ and mocking his prophetic office.

So the witnesses make the verse the mock of Christ's prophetic office. The lesson the expositors draw is the cruelty and the blindness of the mockers, who derided the very gift they could not see, and the patience of Christ under their insult. The witnesses note the cruel sport: "they blindfolded him" — veiling his eyes as in a child's game — and "struck him on the face," then mockingly demanded, "Prophesy, who is it that smote thee?" — challenging him, since he claimed to be a prophet, to name the unseen striker. The expositors observe the point of the mockery, which Ellicott marks: it rested on the popular notion that identified prophecy with clairvoyance, a kind of fortune-telling; the mockers, blindfolding Christ, jeered that the "prophet" could not even tell who struck him, supposing prophecy to be such second-sight. The witnesses press the irony: they mocked as a false prophet the One who was the source of all true prophecy, the Word of God; and the very demand "prophesy who smote thee" was answered, unknowingly, by the fulfilment of his prophecies even then coming to pass — Peter's denial, his own sufferings, all foretold and fulfilling. The expositors draw the lesson on the blindness of the mockers: they could not see that the One they blindfolded saw all things, that the Prophet they derided knew their hearts and their deeds; their mockery exposed their own blindness, not his. The witnesses note Christ's patience under this insult: blindfolded, struck, jeered, he endures in silence, returning no answer to the taunt — the meek endurance of the Lamb led to the slaughter. The expositors observe the depth of the indignity: the holy face of the Son of God veiled and buffeted, the prophetic office mocked, the Truth jeered as a deceiver — humiliation heaped upon humiliation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer, mocked for his faith, is to bear it patiently as Christ did; and to remember that the world's mockery of Christ and his truth springs from its own blindness, not from any defect in him. The verse thus records the mock of Christ's prophetic office — blindfolded and challenged to prophesy — teaching the cruelty and blindness of the mockers, the irony of deriding the source of prophecy, and the patient endurance of Christ under their insult.
65:
"And many other things blasphemously spake they against him." The witnesses note the blasphemies of the mockers and a striking reversal. The Reformed Gill catalogues the abuse: "denying and ridiculing his deity and divine sonship; mocking his offices of prophet, priest, and king; asserting him a mere man, and a very wicked one." John Wesley draws the pointed reversal: "the expression is remarkable. They charged him with blasphemy, because he said he was the Son of God: but the evangelist fixes that charge on them, because he really was so." The witnesses note the mockers heaping many blasphemies on Christ.

The witnesses note the blasphemies of the mockers — and that the true blasphemy was theirs, not his.

So the witnesses make the verse the blasphemies of the mockers. The lesson the expositors draw is that the abuse heaped on Christ was true blasphemy, fixed by the evangelist on the mockers, since Christ truly was the Son of God they reviled. The witnesses note that "many other things blasphemously spake they against him" — the mockers, not content with the blindfolding and blows, poured out blasphemies, reviling Christ in every way; and Gill catalogues the abuse: they denied and ridiculed his deity and divine sonship, mocked his offices, called him a mere man and a wicked one. The expositors press the striking reversal Wesley marks: the word "blasphemously" is remarkable, for the rulers charged Christ with blasphemy because he claimed to be the Son of God; but the evangelist turns the charge upon them — "they blasphemously spake" — for the true blasphemy was theirs, in reviling the One who really was the Son of God. The witnesses observe the irony: to revile a mere man is not blasphemy, for blasphemy is railing against God; but Christ, the mockers' victim, was truly God, and so their abuse of him was real blasphemy, the very crime they falsely charged on him. The expositors draw the lesson on the deity of Christ: the evangelist's word "blasphemously" is itself a confession of Christ's Godhead — for only if Christ is God could the reviling of him be blasphemy; the inspired writer, in calling their abuse blasphemy, affirms that Christ is indeed the Son of God they denied. The witnesses note the depth of the mockers' guilt: not knowing (or refusing to know) whom they reviled, they heaped blasphemy on the Lord of glory, charging him with the very sin they were committing against him. The expositors observe the patience of Christ under blasphemy: he who was reviled, reviled not again (1 Peter 2:23), bearing the blasphemies in silence. The witnesses draw the application: the world's reviling of Christ is real blasphemy, for he is truly God; and the believer is to honour the deity that the mockers denied, and to bear, as Christ did, the reproach of those who blaspheme. The verse thus records the blasphemies of the mockers — fixed by the evangelist as their crime, not Christ's — teaching the deity of Christ confessed in the word "blasphemously," the guilt of those who revile the Son of God, and his patient endurance of their blasphemy.
66:
"And as soon as it was day, the elders of the people and the chief priests and the scribes came together, and led him into their council, saying..." The witnesses note the formal assembly of the Sanhedrin at daybreak. The grammatical-historical Robertson identifies "the council" (the presbyterion, the eldership) as the Sanhedrin, "composed of the elders and scribes and chief priests." Gill and Barnes note the daybreak meeting, the formal session to condemn Christ. The witnesses note the whole council gathering at dawn to try Christ formally.

The witnesses note the formal daybreak session of the Sanhedrin, assembled to condemn Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the convening of the Sanhedrin. The lesson the expositors draw is the formal, official character of the proceeding against Christ — the whole council assembling to give their condemnation the colour of law. The witnesses note the timing: "as soon as it was day" — the night having been spent in the preliminary examinations and the mocking, the formal session of the council convened at daybreak, that the condemnation might appear regular and lawful. The expositors observe the composition Robertson marks: "the elders of the people and the chief priests and the scribes" — the three groups that made up the Sanhedrin, the supreme council of the nation, now gathered in full to try Christ; the whole religious establishment arrayed against him. The witnesses press the official character of the deed: this was no mob action but the formal proceeding of the highest court of Israel, deliberately convened to condemn the Messiah — the rulers of the nation, the guardians of the law and the temple, sitting in judgment on the Lord of glory. The expositors draw the lesson on the guilt of the nation's leaders: it was the official body, the Sanhedrin, that condemned Christ — the nation, through its rulers, rejecting and condemning its Messiah; the guilt not of a few but of the official representatives of Israel. The witnesses note the irony: the council appointed to discern and receive the Messiah now sits to condemn him; the very guardians of the prophecies that foretold him reject their fulfilment. The expositors observe the providence: even this formal trial served the appointed purpose, the rulers fulfilling, in their condemnation, the prophecies of the rejected and suffering Messiah. The witnesses draw the application: the official rejection of Christ by the rulers of Israel is a solemn warning that privilege and position are no security against the deepest sin; those who should have led the nation to its Messiah led it to reject him. The verse thus records the convening of the Sanhedrin at daybreak — the formal assembly to condemn Christ — teaching the official, deliberate character of the proceeding, the guilt of the nation's leaders, and the providence that overruled even their formal condemnation to fulfil the Scriptures.
67:
"Art thou the Christ? tell us. And he said unto them, If I tell you, ye will not believe." The witnesses note the question and Christ's answer, peculiar to Luke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes Luke "passes over the earlier stages — the false witnesses, the charge about the Temple, the silence" — and gives the direct question and answer; "If I tell you, ye will not believe — reported only by St. Luke," and interpreted by John: "Our Lord had told them (John 8:58; 10:30), and they had not believed." Robertson notes the condition assumes their unbelief. The witnesses note Christ's answer exposing the hopelessness of testifying to those resolved not to believe.

The witnesses note Christ's answer: to a question asked in unbelief, his witness would not be received.

So the witnesses make the verse the council's question and Christ's answer. The lesson the expositors draw is the futility of further testimony to those resolved not to believe, and the exposure of the council's settled unbelief. The witnesses note the direct question: "Art thou the Christ? tell us" — the council, abandoning the failed search for witnesses (Ellicott notes Luke omits the false witnesses and the temple-charge), now demands plainly whether Christ claims to be the Messiah. And Christ answers, the expositors observe, not with a bare yes or no, but with a word that exposes their unbelief: "If I tell you, ye will not believe." The witnesses press the meaning Ellicott draws from John: Christ had already told them, again and again, in word and work, who he was (John 8:58; 10:30), and they had refused to believe; so a fresh declaration would be useless, for their unbelief was settled, not from want of evidence but from hardness of heart. The expositors draw the lesson on the hopelessness of testimony to the determined unbeliever: the council asked not to learn but to entrap; they had rejected the abundant evidence already given, and would reject any further word; Christ's answer lays bare that their question was insincere and their unbelief willful. The witnesses note the justice of Christ's reply: he does not refuse to bear witness (he goes on, in the next verses, to affirm his deity), but he first exposes the futility of speaking to those who would not believe — for the fault lay not in the evidence but in their hearts. The expositors observe the abiding truth: where men are resolved not to believe, no testimony, however clear, will convince them; unbelief is finally a matter of the will, not of the want of evidence. The witnesses draw the application: the rejection of Christ by the council warns that the clearest revelation is lost on the hardened heart; and that the responsibility for unbelief lies with the will that refuses, not with any defect in the evidence. The verse thus records the council's question and Christ's answer — "ye will not believe" — teaching the futility of testimony to the determined unbeliever, the exposure of the council's settled, willful unbelief, and the truth that unbelief springs from the heart, not from want of evidence.
68:
"And if I also ask you, ye will not answer me, nor let me go." The witnesses note Christ's exposure of the council's bad faith. The grammatical-historical Ellicott explains: the words "clearly refer to the question He had so recently put to priests and scribes, whether the Christ was the son of David only, or also the Lord of David, which they had been unable to answer" (Matthew 22:41-46); "the words were an indirect protest against their claim to question Him, when they had proved impotent to solve a primary problem." Clarke notes Christ knew they were "determined to put me to death." The witnesses note Christ exposing that the council would neither answer his questions nor release him.

The witnesses note Christ's exposure of the council's bad faith: they would neither answer nor release him.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's exposure of the council's insincerity. The lesson the expositors draw is the bad faith of the council, who questioned Christ without willingness to learn or to deal justly. The witnesses note Christ's word: "if I also ask you, ye will not answer me, nor let me go" — exposing the one-sided injustice of the proceeding; the council demanded answers of him, but would themselves neither answer his questions nor release him whatever he said. The expositors press the reference Ellicott draws out: Christ alludes to the question he had lately put to the rulers — whether the Christ was merely David's son, or also David's Lord (Matthew 22:41-46) — which they had been unable to answer; an indirect protest against their claim to sit in judgment on him, when they could not solve even a primary question about the Messiah. The witnesses observe the injustice this lays bare: the council was no impartial tribunal seeking the truth, but a body resolved on Christ's death; they would not engage his arguments, nor weigh the evidence, nor release him however he answered — their verdict fixed before the trial. The expositors draw the lesson on the bad faith of Christ's judges: they asked questions not to learn but to entrap; they would not be answered, for they had no will to be convinced; and they would not let him go, for they were determined to condemn him. The witnesses note the calm dignity of Christ in exposing this: he lays bare the futility and injustice of the proceeding, not pleading for himself but showing the bad faith of his judges. The expositors observe the contrast: a true tribunal seeks the truth, weighs the evidence, and acquits the innocent; this council sought a pretext, refused all reasoning, and condemned the guiltless. The witnesses draw the application: the trial of Christ exposes the injustice of unbelief, which questions without willingness to learn and judges without willingness to acquit; and warns against the hardened heart that will neither answer the truth nor release its hold on its purpose. The verse thus records Christ's exposure of the council's bad faith — they would neither answer nor release him — teaching the insincerity and injustice of his judges, the futility of dealing with the determined unbeliever, and the calm dignity of Christ laying bare their bad faith.
69:
"Hereafter shall the Son of man sit on the right hand of the power of God." The witnesses note Christ's declaration of his coming exaltation. The grammatical-historical Ellicott renders it "From this time forth shall the Son of Man be sitting," and notes Luke's form is "shall be sitting," not "ye shall see." Clarke reads "hereafter" as "from this very time — the kingdom of God is now going to be set up." Barnes cross-references the session at God's right hand (Hebrews 1:3; 8:1; Revelation 3:21). The witnesses note Christ, the seeming prisoner, declaring his enthronement at the right hand of God.

The witnesses note the great declaration: the condemned Son of man to sit at the right hand of God's power.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's declaration of his exaltation. The lesson the expositors draw is the majestic claim of Christ, the seeming prisoner, to be enthroned at the right hand of God — his coming exaltation declared in the hour of his humiliation. The witnesses note the declaration: "Hereafter shall the Son of man sit on the right hand of the power of God" — Christ, bound and condemned before the council, announces that he shall be exalted to the highest place, seated at God's right hand, the position of supreme honour, authority, and power. The expositors press the contrast: the One who stood as a prisoner, mocked and about to be crucified, declares his enthronement in glory; the depth of his humiliation is met by the height of his exaltation. The witnesses observe Ellicott's note on the form: Luke gives "from this time forth shall the Son of man be sitting" — marking that the exaltation begins now, from the very hour of his suffering; his death and resurrection the gateway to his enthronement, so that "hereafter" means from this time on, the kingdom now to be established. The expositors draw the lesson on the claim of deity and dominion: to sit at the right hand of the power of God is to share the divine throne, the place of universal dominion (Psalm 110:1; Daniel 7:13-14); Christ claims it for himself, the Son of man exalted to reign. The witnesses note the boldness of the confession before the council: Christ does not hide his glory to escape condemnation, but openly declares his exaltation — that the very judges who condemn him shall see him enthroned as their Lord and Judge. The expositors observe the turning of the tables: the prisoner becomes the Judge; the condemned shall be exalted to the throne; those who try him shall stand before him. The witnesses draw the application: the believer is to behold, in the condemned Christ, the exalted Lord enthroned at God's right hand; and to take comfort that the One who suffered for us now reigns in power, our Advocate and King. The verse thus records Christ's declaration of his exaltation — the Son of man at God's right hand — teaching his majestic claim in the hour of humiliation, the height of his exaltation answering the depth of his suffering, and the enthronement of the condemned Christ as Lord and Judge.
70:
"Then said they all, Art thou then the Son of God? And he said unto them, Ye say that I am." The witnesses note the council's question and Christ's confession of his deity. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes "the question, as asked by the whole company, is given only by St. Luke," and that "Ye say that I am" is "complete in itself, but implies, as in the less ambiguous forms in Matthew and Mark, the confession that He actually was what they had asked; the 'I am' has something of the same significance as in John 8:58." Clarke notes they all insisted on this answer. The witnesses note Christ's affirmation that he is the Son of God.

The witnesses note the great confession: Christ owning, before the council, that he is the Son of God.

So the witnesses make the verse Christ's confession of his deity before the council. The lesson the expositors draw is the open avowal of Christ's divine Sonship, sealing his own condemnation by the truth. The witnesses note the council's question, prompted by his declaration of enthronement: "Art thou then the Son of God?" — the whole company (Ellicott notes Luke alone records this) seizing on his claim, demanding whether he asserts the divine Sonship. And Christ answers, "Ye say that I am" — an affirmation, the expositors observe, complete in itself: "ye say" what is true; I am what ye say. The witnesses press Ellicott's note: the form, though it sounds indirect, is a clear confession, equivalent to the plainer affirmations in Matthew and Mark; and the "I am" carries an echo of the divine name (as in John 8:58), Christ owning himself the Son of God. The expositors draw the lesson on the boldness of the confession: Christ does not evade the question to save his life, but openly affirms his deity — knowing that this very confession would be the ground of his condemnation; he seals his death by speaking the truth, confessing his Godhead before the tribunal that would crucify him for it. The witnesses observe the solemnity: the Son of God, on trial before men, confesses who he is — the supreme witness to his own deity, given at the cost of his life. The expositors note the contrast with Peter: where Peter, to save himself, denied his Lord, Christ, to honour the truth, confessed himself the Son of God, though it cost him the cross — the faithful Witness over against the fearful disciple. The witnesses draw the application: the confession of Christ's deity is the heart of the faith, made by Christ himself at the cost of his life; and the believer is to confess, with his Lord, that Jesus is the Son of God, whatever it cost. The verse thus records Christ's confession of his deity before the council — "Ye say that I am" — teaching the open avowal of his divine Sonship, the boldness that sealed his condemnation by the truth, and the supreme witness of Christ to his own Godhead.
71:
"And they said, What need we any further witness? for we ourselves have heard of his own mouth." The witnesses note the council seizing the confession as their ground of condemnation. Clarke explains their reasoning: "we have heard him profess himself the Son of God; he is therefore guilty of blasphemy, and... we must proceed against and condemn him to death" — and, the Jews having no power to execute, "he must now be brought before Pilate." The witnesses note the council, having Christ's own confession, declaring no further witness needed.

The witnesses note the council's verdict: needing no more witnesses, they condemn Christ on his own confession.

So the witnesses make the verse the council's seizing of Christ's confession for condemnation. The lesson the expositors draw is that the council condemned Christ, not on any proven crime, but on his own true confession that he was the Son of God — counting the truth blasphemy. The witnesses note the council's triumphant conclusion: "What need we any further witness? for we ourselves have heard of his own mouth" — the search for false witnesses having failed (as the other Gospels record), the council now finds in Christ's own confession the charge they sought; they need no more, for he has, in their reckoning, condemned himself. The expositors press Clarke's account of their reasoning: they heard Christ profess himself the Son of God, judged this blasphemy, and on that ground resolved to condemn him to death — and, since the Jews had no power to execute, to bring him before Pilate. The witnesses observe the dreadful injustice: the council condemned Christ not for any crime proven by witnesses, but for confessing the truth — that he was the Son of God; the very truth of his deity, which should have been received with worship, was made the ground of his death-sentence, counted blasphemy. The expositors draw the lesson on the perversity of unbelief: the rulers, resolved to condemn, turned Christ's truest confession into his condemnation; what was in fact the highest truth they judged the deepest blasphemy, because their hearts were closed against him. The witnesses note the fulfilment of prophecy: Christ condemned as a blasphemer for confessing his deity, the suffering Servant numbered with transgressors (v.37), going to the death the council now decreed. The expositors observe the transition this marks: the religious trial complete, the council now hands Christ to Pilate for the civil sentence and execution — the chapter closing with the condemnation that would issue in the cross. The witnesses draw the application: the condemnation of Christ for confessing the truth warns that unbelief, resolved against him, will turn even the clearest truth into a charge; and that Christ went to his death for the very confession of his deity that is the foundation of our faith. The verse thus records the council's seizing of Christ's confession for condemnation — needing no further witness — teaching that Christ was condemned on the truth of his own confession, the perversity of unbelief that counted his deity blasphemy, and the transition to the cross that his confession sealed.
